observations: RedactionEngine reads its rule set from .specstory/config/redaction-config.yaml, meaning redaction behavior is fully externalized and can be changed without code deployment, but an invalid config can block all persistence if LSLConfigValidator rejects it

LSLConfigValidator acts as a gating dependency for RedactionEngine: content only flows through redaction after the config schema has been validated, preventing partially-configured redaction rules from silently passing sensitive data

RedactionEngine is applied before persistence, placing it downstream of TranscriptAdapter.convertToLSL() (in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js) and upstream of any file-write or SessionWindowManager routing step

Because TranscriptAdapter.watchTranscripts() tracks only entry counts (not content hashes), RedactionEngine must be idempotent — re-processing the same entry on restart should produce identical redacted output to avoid inconsistent persisted state

The YAML-based configuration implies RedactionEngine supports declarative pattern definitions (likely regex or keyword lists) rather than hard-coded rules, making it extensible for new secret types without modifying engine code

RedactionEngine operates on LSL-format content (post-convertToLSL()), not raw transcript text, meaning redaction patterns must be expressed against the LSL schema's field structure rather than free-form conversation strings

entities.name: LSLClassificationLayer
observations: The authoritative design for all five classification layers is captured in docs/puml/lsl-5-layer-classification.puml, making the PlantUML diagram the primary architectural specification a developer should read first

Classification input is the typed LSL entry taxonomy (user, assistant, tool_use, tool_result, system, error) produced by convertToLSL() in transcript-api.js, so the layer is deliberately decoupled from agent-native formats

The 5-layer model implies a sequential or hierarchical classification pipeline where each layer adds metadata annotations to LSL entries rather than transforming their content, preserving the original entry for auditability

Session-level classification (as opposed to entry-level) means the layer must aggregate signals across an entire hourly bucket produced by SessionWindowManager before assigning top-level session categories

The clean separation enforced by the TranscriptAdapter contract in transcript-api.js ensures LSLClassificationLayer never receives unconverted, agent-specific payloads, which keeps classifier logic stable across new agent backends

entities.name: LLMProxyBridge
observations: `@rapid/llm-proxy` is documented as a shared package across the OKB and coding systems, meaning provider-routing logic is centralised rather than duplicated per integration
The architecture diagram `docs/puml/llm-cli-proxy-architecture.puml` shows the proxy acting as a single HTTP ingress point, decoupling all upstream callers from direct provider SDK dependencies
Five distinct provider backends are supported (Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq, local/DMR, mock), implying a provider-dispatch table or strategy pattern inside the proxy package
The mock provider endpoint allows the proxy to serve deterministic responses during testing without any external network calls, consistent with the `llm-mock-service.ts` mock mode
As a standalone HTTP service, `@rapid/llm-proxy` can be scaled or replaced independently of the MCP server, with the knowledge base explicitly documenting it as shared infrastructure

entities.name: LLMModeController
observations: `getLLMMode()` in `llm-mock-service.ts` evaluates `llmState.perAgentOverrides` first, making per-agent configuration the highest-priority tier; only if no override exists for the requesting agent does it fall through to the lower tiers

`setAgentLLMMode` writes entries into `llmState.perAgentOverrides`, enabling mixed-mode workflows where distinct agents (e.g., a code-indexing agent vs. a complex reasoning agent) are routed to different LLM backends — Docker Model Runner or Anthropic/OpenAI — within a single workflow execution

`setGlobalLLMMode` sets `llmState.globalMode`, acting as the intermediate fallback tier in `getLLMMode()` when no per-agent entry exists in `perAgentOverrides`, covering all agents not individually overridden

`getLLMState` exposes the full `llmState` object (including both `globalMode` and the complete `perAgentOverrides` map) for read-only inspection, allowing callers to audit current assignments without triggering mode resolution side-effects
State is persisted to the shared `workflow-progress.json` file, meaning LLM mode assignments survive process restarts and are visible to every component that reads that file across a workflow run

The legacy `mockLLM` boolean is the lowest-priority tier in `getLLMMode()`: it only activates when both `llmState.perAgentOverrides` and `llmState.globalMode` are absent, preserving backward compatibility for configuration files that predate the `llmState` structure without requiring migration

A developer debugging unexpected mode behavior must inspect all three tiers in order — `perAgentOverrides`, then `globalMode`, then `mockLLM` — because a populated `mockLLM` field in a persisted state file will silently lose to either of the higher-priority fields if they are set

entities.name: TierRouter
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md defines the tiered model selection strategy, distinguishing tiers by task complexity so that lightweight tasks avoid expensive frontier models

docs/puml/llm-tier-routing.puml provides a PlantUML sequence diagram showing TierRouter sitting architecturally above individual provider adapters, receiving a routing request and emitting a (provider, model) pair
The tier routing decision is made after LLMStateManager resolves the operational mode, meaning TierRouter only activates for 'local' and 'public' modes where multiple model sizes exist

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md describes agent identity as an input to tier selection, allowing specific agents to be pinned to a tier regardless of task complexity
TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md references config/llm-providers.yaml as the source of available models per tier, keeping tier definitions data-driven rather than hardcoded

entities.name: LocalLLMFallback
observations: `docs/puml/local-llm-fallback.puml` diagrams the fallback flow, showing that local DMR inference is activated both by explicit `'local'` mode assignment and by public provider failure, making it serve dual roles as a mode target and a resilience mechanism

The `LocalLLMFallback` integrates with `LLMModeController`: when `getLLMMode()` resolves to `'local'`, the request path bypasses public provider dispatch entirely and routes directly to DMR via `@rapid/llm-proxy`
DMR (local inference) appears as one of the five providers in `LLMProxyBridge`, meaning the fallback does not bypass the proxy layer — it changes which provider endpoint the proxy targets
The fallback mechanism allows workflows configured for `'public'` mode to degrade to local inference without code changes, supporting offline development and network-isolated environments

entities.name: ApiServiceWrapper
observations: scripts/api-service.js calls ProcessStateManager.registerService() after the child process is spawned, meaning the service is live before PSM acknowledgment occurs
Any exception thrown by ProcessStateManager.registerService() in scripts/api-service.js is explicitly caught and swallowed, allowing the API service to continue running even if PSM is unavailable
scripts/api-service.js binds a process exit handler that calls PSM.unregisterService() to remove PID/port records, but this cleanup is also best-effort and will not execute on hard crashes
The wrapper treats PSM as a coordination facility rather than a startup gate, meaning the API service will start and serve traffic regardless of PSM state

entities.name: GraphKnowledgeExporter
observations: GraphKnowledgeExporter subscribes to entity:stored events emitted by GraphDatabaseService, making exports eventually consistent with writes rather than synchronously blocking them
Debouncing is applied to export writes, batching rapid successive entity:stored events into a single file write — this prevents write storms during bulk batch analysis pipeline runs
Exports are partitioned into per-domain JSON files under .data/knowledge-export, allowing consumers to load only the domain-relevant slice of the knowledge graph
Because exports are JSON files on disk, they serve as a portable snapshot readable by external tools (RAG integrations, code-graph-rag, MCP servers) without requiring a running VKB server

The exporter's event-driven design means it is decoupled from both ManualLearning and OnlineLearning write paths — any write that triggers entity:stored will be reflected in exports regardless of origin

entities.name: OntologyClassificationEngine
observations: The engine lives inside PersistenceAgent and wraps @fwornle/km-core's OntologyRegistry, meaning ontology classification is a persistence-time concern rather than an extraction-time concern
A 5-minute TTL classification cache prevents redundant LLM invocations for entity patterns seen repeatedly during high-throughput batch ingestion from OnlineLearning
Three classification methods — 'heuristic', 'llm', and 'hybrid' — allow callers to trade classification latency against accuracy depending on context (e.g., bulk migration vs. interactive editing)
Confidence scoring on classification results enables downstream consumers to gate on certainty thresholds, for example skipping low-confidence ontology assignments during KmCoreMigrationPipeline runs

The engine's placement within PersistenceAgent mirrors the pattern described for PersistenceAgent.mapEntityToSharedMemory(), where ontology metadata fields are pre-populated to prevent re-classification on subsequent reads

entities.name: KmCoreMigrationPipeline
observations: scripts/migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs is the primary migration runner, operating as a standalone script rather than an in-process module to avoid holding the LevelDB file lock simultaneously with the running server
src/migration/migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.test.ts provides integration test coverage for the migration, implying the transformation logic is testable in isolation from the live graph database
UUIDv7 ID assignment during migration establishes time-ordered canonical identifiers for all legacy blobs, enabling chronological traversal and deduplication in the post-migration graph
Provenance stamps added by the migration distinguish pre-existing legacy entities from entities created after the km-core adoption, preserving audit lineage in the graph

The error-budget exit policy — aborting migration after a configurable threshold of transformation failures — prevents silent partial migrations where some entities land in km-core shape while others remain as legacy blobs in the same graph

entities.name: AgentConstructionPattern
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md documents the agent architecture showing each agent follows a constructor + lazy-init + execute() lifecycle rather than eager initialization at import time

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md (now RESOLVED) identified that bypassing ensureLLMInitialized() caused runtime failures, confirming this method is the single gate for provider readiness

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md describes how the lazy initialization pattern allows agents to be instantiated without an active LLM connection, enabling unit testing and dry-run modes

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md specifies that LLM provider credentials are resolved inside ensureLLMInitialized() by reading config/llm-providers.yaml, decoupling agent construction from environment state

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/tools.md describes Tool Extensions that plug into the execute() phase, meaning the lifecycle contract is also the extension point for instrumentation

entities.name: ConfigDrivenBehavior
observations: config/agent-profiles.json defines per-agent behavioral parameters (e.g., which LLM tier to use, concurrency limits) so adding a new agent type requires only a new JSON entry, not a code change
config/health-verification-rules.json externalizes health check logic so rules can be disabled during incidents by editing JSON without triggering a code review cycle

config/llm-providers.yaml maps provider names to model tiers and endpoints, making provider switching a config-only operation as documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md

config/knowledge-management.json and config/hooks-config.json extend the pattern to knowledge pipelines and hook definitions, establishing that every new subsystem must declare its configurable surface in a corresponding config file

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md explicitly states that environment variables alone are insufficient — structured config files are the canonical source of behavioral parameters
integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/constraint-configuration.md demonstrates the pattern for the constraint monitor subsystem, showing a dedicated config document per integration

entities.name: HookExtensionSystem
observations: integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md specifies the exact JSON payload format that hooks emit on each tool call entry and exit, defining the contract between agents and monitors
config/hooks-config.json registers hook handlers by event type (pre-tool, post-tool) and target agent, so new instrumentation is added by editing this file rather than inserting code into agents

integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/status-line-integration.md shows that the status-line update feature is implemented purely as a hook handler, demonstrating that hooks are the intended extension mechanism for cross-cutting concerns

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/tools.md describes Tool Extensions as the in-process complement to hooks, clarifying that hooks handle out-of-process observers while tool extensions handle in-process augmentation
integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md describes the constraint monitor as a hook consumer, meaning the entire constraint enforcement subsystem is non-invasive by architectural convention

entities.name: MCPToolExposurePattern
observations: integrations/code-graph-rag/README.md describes the code-graph-rag system exposing its graph query capabilities as MCP tools, not as a Python library import or REST API
integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/README.md establishes that semantic analysis capabilities are consumed by agents through MCP tool calls, with docs/api/README.md listing each exposed tool
integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md shows the constraint monitor follows the same pattern — constraint checking is an MCP server, not a shared library
integrations/code-graph-rag/docs/claude-code-setup.md provides setup instructions wiring the MCP server into Claude Code, confirming MCP is the uniform agent-facing interface layer

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md documents Integration Patterns, explicitly describing how agents discover and call MCP tools rather than importing service modules
integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md (RESOLVED) listed direct service coupling as a critical issue, confirming MCP-only access is an enforced architectural rule

entities.name: DiagramFirstDocumentation
observations: docs/puml/_standard-style.puml provides shared color palette, font, and stereotype definitions imported by all other diagrams, ensuring visual consistency across subsystem diagrams
integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md references PlantUML diagrams as the primary architecture artifacts, with prose documentation as secondary explanation
The llm-tier-routing.puml diagram (referenced in TieredLLMRouting) exemplifies the convention: routing logic is documented as a diagram before being encoded in config/llm-providers.yaml
integrations/code-graph-rag/CONTRIBUTING.md includes diagram update requirements in the contribution guidelines, making diagram maintenance a gated step in the development workflow
The docs/puml/ directory structure mirrors the subsystem layout, so a developer can navigate diagrams by the same mental model as the codebase directories

entities.name: TieredLLMRouting
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md formally proposes and documents the tiered model selection approach, classifying tasks into complexity buckets before provider assignment
config/llm-providers.yaml maps each provider to a tier label (e.g., local, remote, fast, capable) so routing decisions reference tier names rather than hard-coded model strings
config/agent-profiles.json references tier names per agent task class, meaning changing a task from lightweight to heavyweight routing requires only a JSON edit, not code changes
integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md references llm-tier-routing.puml as the canonical diagram showing decision flow from task complexity to provider selection

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md documents how the ensureLLMInitialized() method reads the tier assignment from config and resolves the actual provider endpoint at runtime

entities.name: McpConstraintMonitor
observations: integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md defines the top-level purpose of this integration, positioning it as the runtime monitoring surface for constraints detected by the underlying system.
integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/README.md indicates a dedicated dashboard sub-component exists for visualizing constraint status, separate from the core detection logic.

integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/status-line-integration.md documents how constraint state is surfaced in a status line UI, suggesting the monitor emits real-time state updates consumable by editor or terminal integrations.
integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md defines the data contract between Claude Code hook events and the monitor, acting as the integration boundary specification.

The monitor hosts both SemanticConstraintDetector docs and configuration docs within its docs/ directory, indicating it is the deployment unit that composes detection modes and configuration into a running service.

entities.name: ConstraintDashboard
observations: integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/README.md documents the dashboard as a separate deployable under the dashboard/ subdirectory, indicating it is architecturally decoupled from the MCP server process
The dashboard sources data exclusively from the violation history JSON store written by McpConstraintMonitor, making it a read-only consumer with no direct hook pipeline involvement
Severity breakdown display implies the violation history schema includes a severity field, which would be populated by either the pattern-based or semantic detection layers upstream
Session statistics tracking suggests the dashboard groups violations by session identifier present in Claude Code hook payloads as documented in docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md

entities.name: LSLMetadataWindowField
observations: The parent L2 description explicitly names transcript-api.js and lsl-converter.js as the two files that define LSLMetadata with a session-window field, establishing that the field is declared in at least two places and must be kept in sync.
The 'HHMM-HHMM' format (e.g., '0900-1000') encodes both the bucket start and end time in a single string, making the field self-describing and sortable without requiring a secondary timestamp lookup.

Because the field appears in lsl-converter.js as well as transcript-api.js, it bridges the raw transcript representation and the LSL output layer, meaning any change to the window format must be propagated across both files to avoid routing mismatches.

The field functions as a routing key: downstream consumers of LSLMetadata in SessionWindowManager use this string directly to derive the target file path for a session, so an absent or malformed window value would result in an unresolvable write destination.

entities.name: HourlyBucketResolver
observations: The L2 parent context states that SessionWindowManager 'must compute and assign' the window field at write time, confirming that bucket resolution is not pre-computed at session creation but is deferred until the moment of the write operation.

The resolver must truncate or floor the incoming timestamp to the top of the current hour to generate the start segment of the 'HHMM-HHMM' string, then add exactly 60 minutes for the end segment — a deterministic arithmetic operation with no external dependencies.

Because lsl-converter.js and transcript-api.js both carry the session-window field in LSLMetadata, the resolver's output must conform to the same format contract used by both files, making the 'HHMM-HHMM' pattern the single source of truth for bucket identity.

The resolved window string doubles as a file-path segment, so the resolver is also implicitly responsible for filesystem-safe formatting — the zero-padded four-digit time components (e.g., '0900') satisfy this requirement without additional sanitization.

entities.name: PatternScrubPass
observations: Because redaction rules originate in .specstory/config/redaction-config.yaml (per the parent component description), the scrub pass must be decoupled from rule definitions and accept an injected rule set, enabling the config to be swapped without touching scrubbing logic.

The parent context positions redaction as a pre-persistence concern within the LiveLoggingSystem pipeline, meaning the scrub pass must operate inline on each LSL entry before any write occurs rather than as a post-hoc sanitization step.

Supporting three distinct sensitive-pattern categories (tokens, keys, PII) documented in the parent context suggests the pass applies multiple compiled matchers per entry field, with each matcher tied to a specific redaction placeholder format.

entities.name: FiveLayerClassificationSpec
observations: The SubComponent (L2) description explicitly designates docs/puml/lsl-5-layer-classification.puml as 'the primary architectural specification a developer should read first', making it the single source of truth for all classification layer definitions rather than any runtime code module.

The diagram encodes exactly five classification layers, as called out in the SubComponent description ('all five classification layers'), suggesting the taxonomy has a fixed, enumerated structure that downstream consumers depend on for schema stability.

Per the parent analysis, both the entry-level classifier (per-entry label assignment) and the session-level aggregator (session-scoped rollup) reference this diagram as their shared output contract, meaning any schema change in the PlantUML propagates as a breaking change to both layers.

The fact that the spec lives in docs/puml/ (a documentation path, not a source path) indicates an architecture-first design discipline where the diagram is kept canonical and code is expected to conform to it, rather than the diagram being generated from code.

entities.name: AgentModeOverrideStore
observations: setAgentLLMMode writes a keyed entry into .data/workflow-progress.json whose key identifies the agent and whose value is the chosen mode; getLLMState in llm-mock-service.ts looks up this key before consulting the global mode field, establishing agent entries as the highest-priority tier.

Because the store is backed by a flat JSON file rather than in-process memory, agent-level overrides survive across getLLMState() calls and can be inspected or modified externally (e.g., by test harnesses or operators) without touching service code.

The three-value mode enum used for stored values supersedes the older mockLLM boolean that earlier callers may have written, so getLLMState must accommodate both representations when reading the per-agent entries from workflow-progress.json.

entities.name: LegacyMockFlag
observations: `getLLMMode()` in `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts` consults `mockLLM` only after both the per-agent store and `llmState.globalMode` are found unset, placing it third in a four-level chain that bottoms out at the hardcoded `'public'` string fallback.

Its position at level 3—below two richer mode fields—signals that it was the original configuration mechanism before the system adopted a named-mode taxonomy, and its continued presence is a backward-compatibility shim rather than a first-class design choice.

As a simple boolean, `mockLLM` cannot express the full range of backends the newer mode fields support (e.g., distinguishing between multiple mock variants or provider tiers), which is exactly why `globalMode` and per-agent overrides were introduced above it.

Any new integration code should avoid relying on `mockLLM` and instead use `setGlobalLLMMode` or `setAgentLLMMode`, as the boolean will be shadowed whenever either of those higher-priority mechanisms is set.

entities.name: TierModelMap
observations: The tier-to-model mapping is formally diagrammed in `docs/puml/llm-tier-routing.puml`, establishing it as a distinct routing artifact with its own documented structure rather than being embedded in general provider configuration.

Per the SubComponent description, tier assignment is sequenced before provider selection, meaning TierModelMap is evaluated upstream of the broader provider architecture shown in `docs/puml/llm-provider-architecture.puml` — the map output feeds into that downstream pipeline.

By translating abstract tier identifiers into specific provider/model pairs, TierModelMap decouples callers from knowing which provider or model underlies a given capability tier, enforcing a clean separation between tier semantics and provider implementation details.

entities.name: TierProposalSpec
observations: `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md` (titled 'Tiered Model Selection Proposal') is the authoritative source for why specific tiers exist and what capability or cost tradeoff each tier is intended to represent, providing the design intent behind the tier identifiers used in `docs/puml/llm-tier-routing.puml`.

The spec predates and informs the routing diagram — `docs/puml/llm-tier-routing.puml` diagrams the mechanical mapping while TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md captures the semantic contract (i.e., what each tier means to callers), making it essential reading before modifying tier definitions.

Because the document defines tier semantics rather than implementation, it serves as the specification against which both TierModelMap entries and any tier-assignment logic should be validated when new models or providers are introduced.

entities.name: FallbackTriggerLogic
observations: The `docs/puml/local-llm-fallback.puml` fallback flow diagram exposes two independent entry paths into DMR inference: one triggered by explicit `mode == 'local'` assignment, and one triggered by public provider unavailability — meaning the same local endpoint is reused for both planned and emergency routing.

The OR-condition design (mode == 'local' OR public provider failure) means FallbackTriggerLogic must distinguish between the two trigger origins at evaluation time, since proactive local-mode requests and reactive failure-fallbacks likely require different upstream signaling or error context propagation.

Per the SubComponent (L2) description, this dual-role architecture is a deliberate design decision within `@rapid/llm-proxy`, making FallbackTriggerLogic a single chokepoint through which all DMR-bound traffic flows regardless of cause — centralizing routing policy for local inference in one place rather than scattering fallback logic across provider-specific code paths.

entities.name: DMREndpointConnector
observations: As noted in the SubComponent (L2) context, DMREndpointConnector lives within `@rapid/llm-proxy` and is responsible for the actual network-level connection to the local DMR inference server, making it the execution-side counterpart to FallbackTriggerLogic's decision-side role.

Because `docs/puml/local-llm-fallback.puml` shows local DMR inference as the terminal node reached by both trigger paths, DMREndpointConnector must be designed to handle requests arriving from two distinct upstream contexts (explicit-mode and failure-fallback) without requiring caller-specific branching in its own logic.

The description of DMREndpointConnector as a 'request adapter' implies it normalizes the outgoing request format to match local DMR server expectations — a necessary translation layer if the local inference server exposes a different API contract than the public providers it substitutes for.

entities.name: PsmRegistrationSideEffect
observations: scripts/api-service.js calls ProcessStateManager.registerService() only after the child process has been successfully spawned, as explicitly described in the ApiServiceWrapper sub-component — the service is operationally live before PSM acknowledgment occurs.

This ordering means that any PSM consumer (e.g., a health check, routing layer, or orchestrator) polling immediately after spawn may observe the service as unregistered despite it already accepting traffic, a race condition flagged as the defining architectural concern of ApiServiceWrapper.

Because registration is a side effect triggered by a completed spawn rather than a precondition for it, failures in ProcessStateManager.registerService() would leave a running child process in a permanently unregistered state from PSM's perspective.

entities.name: ChildProcessSpawner
observations: scripts/api-service.js uses Node's child_process.spawn (not exec or fork) to launch the Express API, which keeps the child process lifetime directly observable through process lifecycle events (e.g., 'exit', 'error') on the returned ChildProcess handle.

The spawn call in api-service.js deliberately separates the OS-level PID of the Express child from the service identity registered with PSM — the wrapper process owns the PSM identity while the child owns the actual port binding.

By positioning api-service.js as the spawn parent rather than running the Express API directly, the architecture allows the wrapper to intercept stdout/stderr streams and proxy lifecycle events before they reach PSM or any external observer.

entities.name: PostSpawnPsmRegistration
observations: scripts/dashboard-service.js explicitly follows the same post-spawn PSM registration pattern as scripts/api-service.js, confirming this is a shared architectural convention across the DockerizedServices component, not an incidental implementation choice.

PSM is characterized as a 'non-blocking coordination sink', meaning the PSM.registerService() call is fire-and-forget: registration failures do not halt, roll back, or otherwise affect the already-running dashboard process.

By deferring PSM registration until after spawn, the design decouples process lifecycle from service-registry availability — the dashboard can start and serve traffic even if PSM is unreachable, with error suppression making this explicit.
The post-spawn ordering establishes a clear sequencing contract: child process launch is the critical path; PSM notification is an optional coordination side effect that trails it.

entities.name: RegistrationApi
observations: ProcessStateManager.registerService() is explicitly identified in the L2 description as the single entry point used by both scripts/api-service.js and scripts/dashboard-service.js, establishing it as the canonical write interface for service identity data.

The parent analysis suggests a paired unregisterService() method, meaning the API follows a symmetric register/unregister lifecycle contract aligned with process start and stop events in the wrapper scripts.

Because registerService() is called after spawn (not before), the API implicitly guarantees that a PID is available at call time — callers must have a live process handle before invoking it, making PID a required rather than optional registration field.

The two callers (api-service.js and dashboard-service.js) consuming an identical API surface indicates the registration contract is intentionally service-agnostic, decoupling ProcessStateManager from any knowledge of what each service does.

entities.name: EntityAuthoringInterface
observations: GraphDatabaseAdapter (named in the ManualLearning parent description) acts as the entry point for all manual entity writes, distinguishing human-authored mutations from automated pipeline writes before routing decisions are made.
All manual entity edits are forwarded through VkbApiClient REST endpoints, ensuring the server-owned LevelDB instance remains the single authoritative store for human-curated knowledge nodes.
The interface enforces the constraint that human-authored edits never bypass the VKB HTTP server — preventing a competing direct-connection write path that could diverge from server state.

entities.name: VkbServerAvailabilityGate
observations: GraphDatabaseAdapter (referenced explicitly in the ManualLearning sub-component description) performs a server-availability probe prior to each manual write, selecting the REST path only when the VKB HTTP server is confirmed up.

This gate exists specifically to prevent a competing direct LevelDB connection from being opened alongside the server-owned instance, a scenario the parent description calls out as an architectural risk.
When the VKB HTTP server is unavailable, the gate acts as a write-blocking control point rather than silently falling back to a direct connection, preserving consistency of human-authored data.

entities.name: VkbApiClient
observations: VkbApiClient is located at lib/ukb-unified/core/VkbApiClient.js and is dynamically imported at runtime, so callers must handle the case where the import fails (server not running) and fall back to direct LevelDB access

The client provides lock-free entity CRUD, meaning concurrent readers and writers do not block each other — this is the primary architectural reason for preferring the HTTP API over direct file access
Dynamic import strategy means VkbApiClient has no static dependency in the module graph, keeping the core KnowledgeManagement code runnable in offline/embedded scenarios
All six canonical entity types (Project, Component, SubComponent, Pattern, Detail, System) are managed through the same VkbApiClient CRUD interface, with entity type encoded in the request payload

The client communicates with a server that fronts the Graphology + LevelDB stack, so all writes through VkbApiClient propagate to both the in-memory graph and persistent storage atomically from the client's perspective

entities.name: AdapterBoundExporterLifecycle
observations: The parent context explicitly states that GraphDatabaseAdapter in storage/graph-database-adapter.ts attaches the exporter at initialization, making the exporter's start and teardown implicit consequences of adapter construction and destruction rather than independently orchestrated events.

Because the exporter is not independently managed, there is no separate start/stop API surface for export synchronization — downstream consumers must control the adapter itself to influence export behavior, a notable constraint for testing or partial-lifecycle scenarios.

This coupling means any failure or restart of the GraphDatabaseAdapter will implicitly reset the export sync state, which is an architectural risk if the adapter is ephemeral but the exported .json files are meant to be durably maintained.

entities.name: MutationDebouncer
observations: The parent context describes the GraphKnowledgeExporter as managing an 'export sync lifecycle' tied to storage/graph-database-adapter.ts, which implies an internal event accumulation mechanism that must coalesce mutations before triggering writes.

Debounce-style scheduling — where a quiet period must elapse before flushing — is the canonical pattern for this category of graph exporter, preventing write amplification when many nodes or edges are added in rapid succession (e.g., during bulk ingestion).

Because the exporter's lifecycle is bound to the adapter (storage/graph-database-adapter.ts), the MutationDebouncer's pending queue must also be flushed or abandoned on adapter teardown, making its shutdown path a critical correctness boundary.

entities.name: OntologyRegistryBridge
observations: The SubComponent description explicitly states the OntologyClassificationEngine 'wraps @fwornle/km-core's OntologyRegistry', establishing that OntologyRegistry is the authoritative external classification source and a translation layer is required between PersistenceAgent's internal entity model and the OntologyRegistry API boundary.

Positioning classification inside PersistenceAgent (persistence-time) rather than in the extraction pipeline (extraction-time) means the bridge owns the impedance mismatch: it must transform PersistenceAgent's entity structures into whatever input format @fwornle/km-core's OntologyRegistry expects before a class can be assigned.

This design deliberately decouples the extraction pipeline from ontology schema changes — updates to @fwornle/km-core's OntologyRegistry contract are absorbed by the bridge alone, leaving extraction-side code unchanged.

Because the bridge sits inside PersistenceAgent, it inherits PersistenceAgent's lifecycle and transaction context, meaning classification lookups are co-located with write operations rather than being a separate pre-write step.

entities.name: OutOfProcessMigrationRunner
observations: scripts/migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs is the single entry point for the migration, implemented as a top-level .mjs ES-module script rather than an importable library — this design choice is explicitly documented in the component description to avoid dual file-lock ownership between the migration and the live server process.

Because LevelDB enforces a single-writer file lock, running the migration in-process alongside the server would cause the server to either crash or fail to open its own store; the standalone-script pattern sidesteps this by ensuring only one process holds the lock at a time.

The .mjs extension signals native ES-module semantics (top-level await, import/export), which is consistent with a self-contained runner that orchestrates its own async pipeline without needing to be wired into a CommonJS require graph.

entities.name: ServerAvailabilityProbe
observations: The probe is executed at adapter initialization in storage/graph-database-adapter.ts, meaning the storage backend decision is made once per adapter lifetime rather than per-request, avoiding repeated network checks during entity reads and writes.
The probe invokes VkbApiClient.isServerAvailable(), a method on the HTTP REST client, making the VKB server process the source of truth for which process holds the LevelDB file lock.

A positive probe result activates the REST path (all entity operations are delegated to VkbApiClient), while a negative result activates the direct LevelDB path via GraphDatabaseService — these two paths are mutually exclusive within one adapter instance.

entities.name: DynamicVkbApiClientImport
observations: The dynamic import of VkbApiClient is conditional on the result of VkbApiClient.isServerAvailable(), so the REST client module is not loaded at all when the adapter is running in direct LevelDB mode, reducing initialization overhead.

When VkbApiClient is active, it proxies all entity reads and writes to the VKB server process, meaning the adapter process never acquires the LevelDB file lock itself — the lock remains exclusively with the server process.

GraphDatabaseService, the direct LevelDB accessor, is instantiated only when VkbApiClient is absent (server unavailable), at which point the current process takes ownership of the LevelDB file lock for the duration of the adapter's lifetime.

entities.name: ExecuteOrchestration
observations: Documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md, `execute()` is the third phase of the agent lifecycle (after constructor and lazy-init), representing the standard interface all agents expose for triggering their analysis or processing work.

Wave agents implement `execute()` to sequence multiple tool calls in a defined order, meaning orchestration logic (which tools to call, in what order, with what inputs) lives inside this method rather than being externally driven by the caller.

Because lazy initialization happens inside `execute()` (via `ensureLLMInitialized()`) rather than before it, the method combines two responsibilities: ensuring provider readiness and running the actual tool-call pipeline, which simplifies the external call site to a single `agent.execute()` invocation.

The consistent `execute()` contract across all agents enables the MCP server to treat different agent types polymorphically, invoking them through a shared interface regardless of the specific wave or tool sequence each implements.

entities.name: AgentProfileSchema
observations: Based on the parent context, each entry in config/agent-profiles.json governs runtime behavior including which LLM tier to use and concurrency limits for a given agent type.
The schema follows a config-driven pattern documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md, where agent behavioral parameters are externalized from code.

The tiered model selection pattern referenced in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md aligns with agent-profiles.json fields that map agents to LLM tier classifications.

entities.name: HookPayloadFormat
observations: Documented in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md (listed as 'Claude Code Hook Data Format' in Project Documentation), this file is the canonical specification for the hook payload — a new developer should treat it as the source of truth for any hook consumer or producer.

The sub-component description explicitly frames this format as 'the contract between agents and monitors,' meaning the payload schema is an interface boundary: agents emit payloads conforming to this schema, and the MCP Constraint Monitor (integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md) depends on that schema to parse and evaluate tool events.

The format covers both entry (pre-tool-use) and exit (post-tool-use) lifecycle points per tool call, as stated in the sub-component description ('hooks emit on each tool call entry and exit'); this dual-event structure means consumers must handle two distinct payload shapes or a shared schema with an event-type discriminator field.

The constraint-configuration.md and semantic-constraint-detection.md files in the same integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/ directory depend downstream on this payload format — constraint rules and semantic detection logic both reference the fields that CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md defines, so changes to the payload schema have cascading effects across those modules.

entities.name: SseTransportMCPBinding
observations: The project documentation enumerates two separate port configuration keys, CODE_GRAPH_RAG_PORT and CODE_GRAPH_RAG_SSE_PORT, indicating the server intentionally separates standard and SSE transport modes rather than using a single endpoint.

integrations/code-graph-rag/docs/claude-code-setup.md ('Claude Code Setup for Graph-Code MCP Server') is dedicated entirely to configuring Claude Code to connect to this MCP server, confirming that SSE transport is the primary integration path for AI assistant clients.

The README (integrations/code-graph-rag/README.md, 'Graph-Code: A Graph-Based RAG System for Any Codebases') positions the system as an MCP server rather than a library or REST service, making the dual-port SSE binding a first-class architectural concern rather than an afterthought.

entities.name: SharedPlantUmlStylePalette
observations: `docs/puml/_standard-style.puml` is explicitly described in the parent sub-component as the single source of shared color palette, font, and stereotype definitions, meaning all other `.puml` files depend on it via an `!include` or `!import` directive rather than duplicating style rules.

The underscore-prefixed filename (`_standard-style.puml`) follows the PlantUML convention for partial/shared fragments that are not intended to be rendered as standalone diagrams, signaling to maintainers that this file is an infrastructure include, not a deliverable diagram.

Because every subsystem diagram imports this file, any global visual change (e.g., rebranding a primary color or adjusting a font size) requires editing only `docs/puml/_standard-style.puml`, and the update propagates automatically to all consuming diagrams without per-file edits.

The stereotype definitions housed in this file allow diagram authors to annotate elements (e.g., `<<service>>`, `<<database>>`) with pre-styled visual treatments, enforcing semantic consistency between diagram type and visual appearance across subsystem documentation.

entities.name: ClaudeCodeHookPayloadFormat
observations: Dedicated documentation at `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md` (titled 'Claude Code Hook Data Format') describes the exact shape of payloads the server receives, making it the canonical interface contract for the hook receiver role described in the parent README.

Because McpConstraintMonitor operates as a downstream endpoint rather than inline with Claude Code (per `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md`), this format document is the primary integration seam — producers (UnifiedHookManager) and the consumer (this server) are decoupled by this payload schema.

The existence of a standalone format document implies the payload structure is versioned or considered stable enough to document separately, which a new developer should treat as the entry point when wiring a new hook source into the monitor.

entities.name: DecoupledDeployableUnit
observations: `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/README.md` serves as the dedicated documentation entrypoint for the dashboard as its own deployable, signaling that it has a distinct lifecycle from the parent MCP Constraint Monitor server

The SubComponent description explicitly states the dashboard is 'architecturally decoupled from the MCP server process,' implying a process-boundary separation where the dashboard and MCP server communicate over a defined interface (e.g., HTTP, WebSocket, or file-based) rather than in-process calls

Placement under `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/` rather than inline within the server source directory establishes a physical boundary in the repository that mirrors the runtime decoupling, making independent build and deployment straightforward for contributors

entities.name: StatusLineIntegrationSurface
observations: `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/status-line-integration.md` is a dedicated document titled 'Status Line Integration,' indicating this is a first-class, explicitly designed output channel of the constraint monitoring system rather than an ad-hoc addition

The existence of a separate doc (`integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md`) titled 'Claude Code Hook Data Format' alongside the status line doc suggests the dashboard ingests structured hook-format events and projects a summarized status outward — the status line being one such projection

Positioning this documentation at the `docs/` level of the mcp-constraint-monitor integration (not inside `dashboard/`) implies the status line surface is shared infrastructure that the decoupled dashboard participates in, not solely owns, pointing to a publish/subscribe or polling pattern between components

entities.name: SemanticDetectionDesignRationale
observations: docs/semantic-detection-design.md ('Semantic Constraint Detection - Design Document') exists as a standalone design document separate from the operational docs/semantic-constraint-detection.md, indicating the semantic detector went through a formal design phase distinct from the pattern-based engine.

The existence of two parallel documents — one operational (semantic-constraint-detection.md) and one design-rationale (semantic-detection-design.md) — suggests the semantic approach introduced novel architectural decisions requiring justification beyond standard pattern matching.
Both documents reside under integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/, tying this design to the MCP Constraint Monitor integration described in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md.

entities.name: SequentialAgentOrchestration
observations: As documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md ('Agent Architecture'), the pipeline explicitly sequences all four agents in a defined order, establishing handoff points between semantic analysis, ontology classification, code graph construction, and content validation stages rather than running them concurrently.

The presence of integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md ('CRITICAL Architecture Issues - RESOLVED') indicates the sequential orchestration pattern resulted from deliberate architectural conflict resolution, implying the agent ordering carries specific design intent and is not arbitrary.

The companion document integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md ('Integration Patterns') addresses how the sequenced agents interoperate within the broader MCP server, suggesting the pipeline enforces integration contracts at each agent boundary to pass intermediate results downstream.

entities.name: CommittedKGInsightConsumer
observations: The SubComponent description explicitly states Insights is 'architecturally downstream of the pipeline's persistence stage, consuming committed KG data rather than raw agent outputs,' meaning insight logic is decoupled from live agent execution and only triggered post-commit.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md (titled 'CRITICAL Architecture Issues - RESOLVED') documents resolved architectural concerns, indicating the boundary between pipeline persistence and downstream insight generation was a subject of explicit architectural scrutiny.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md (titled 'Integration Patterns') likely defines the interface contract between the KG persistence layer and Insights, ensuring the sub-component reads only from a stable, fully-committed graph snapshot.

The KG relationship types visible in project documentation (DEFINES, DEPENDS_ON_EXTERNAL, CONTAINS_FILE, CONTAINS_MODULE) represent the committed schema that Insights queries to derive semantic observations — confirming that graph completeness is a prerequisite for meaningful insight extraction.

entities.name: SharedEmbeddingCore
observations: `src/agents/semantic-analyzer.ts` is explicitly described in the parent context as 'the single point of change for common embedding, similarity, or tokenization logic', confirming that embedding is one of its primary, first-class responsibilities rather than a side concern

Because the file is shared across all four concrete agent subclasses (stated in the L2 description), any change to embedding model selection, dimensionality, or normalization strategy propagates automatically to every agent, making this the highest-leverage location for embedding-related refactors

The architecture documentation at `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md` covers agent architecture that depends on shared semantic analysis capabilities, corroborating that embedding is a cross-cutting concern deliberately extracted into this shared file rather than duplicated per agent

entities.name: SemanticSimilarityComputer
observations: The parent context names 'similarity' as one of three explicit responsibilities of `src/agents/semantic-analyzer.ts` (alongside embedding and tokenization), indicating it is a discrete, named capability rather than an incidental utility

`integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-constraint-detection.md` and `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-detection-design.md` both document semantic constraint detection workflows that rely on similarity scoring, showing that downstream consumers depend on a stable, centralized similarity API matching what `src/agents/semantic-analyzer.ts` provides

Collocating similarity computation with embedding generation in the same file enforces that the same vector space used to produce embeddings is also used to compare them, preventing drift between encoding and scoring strategies across agent subclasses

entities.name: RedactionSubsystem
observations: .specstory/config/redaction-config.yaml organizes redaction rules by named categories, allowing operators to enable or disable entire rule groups without modifying individual patterns
LSLConfigValidator validates the redaction-config.yaml schema at startup, ensuring malformed or missing category rules cause a loud failure rather than silent pass-through of sensitive content

LSLConverter applies redaction during its serialization step, meaning redaction is the last transformation before bytes are written — raw in-memory entries retain original content for classification purposes

The per-category rule structure in redaction-config.yaml implies regex or pattern-based matching per category, giving operators fine-grained control over what constitutes sensitive content in each domain

Because redaction occurs inside LSLConverter serialization rather than at ingestion, the ClassificationPipeline operates on unredacted content, preserving signal quality for multi-layer classification

The coupling between LSLConfigValidator and redaction-config.yaml means configuration hot-reload requires re-validation, a constraint new developers must respect when adding runtime config refresh logic

entities.name: ClassificationPipeline
observations: docs/puml/lsl-5-layer-classification.puml diagrams five discrete classification layers, establishing a strict ordered pipeline where each layer's output can inform subsequent layers rather than operating independently

config/live-logging-config.json provides runtime configuration for the pipeline, likely specifying enabled layers, confidence thresholds, or category vocabularies that control classification behavior without code changes
Classification is applied to entries before LSLConverter serialization/redaction, meaning the 5-layer labels are available as metadata on the entry that redaction and windowing can reference

The 5-layer architecture as described in the PUML diagram implies a separation between syntactic, semantic, intent, context, and output-type classification concerns, each encapsulated in its own layer module

The TranscriptAdapter.watchTranscripts() cursor in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js feeds new entries into the classification pipeline on each polling tick, meaning classification latency is bounded by the setInterval period

Because config/live-logging-config.json governs pipeline behavior, adding a new classification category requires only a config update plus any necessary model or rule assets, not structural code changes

entities.name: TranscriptAdapterFramework
observations: TranscriptAdapter in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js declares five abstract methods — getAgentType(), getTranscriptDirectory(), readTranscripts(), convertToLSL(), and getCurrentSession() — forming the mandatory interface for any new agent integration

The concrete watchTranscripts() method in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js implements a setInterval polling loop, centralizing the scheduling mechanism so subclasses cannot accidentally diverge in polling behavior

A lastEntryCount cursor maintained by watchTranscripts() slices only newly appended entries on each interval tick, making the deduplication strategy O(new entries) rather than O(total entries) per poll
The cursor-based approach is optimized for append-only log files; it would fail to detect deletions or out-of-order insertions, a documented constraint new adapter authors should understand

Adding a new agent integration (e.g., for an agent other than Claude Code) requires only subclassing TranscriptAdapter and implementing the five abstract methods — the polling lifecycle is inherited without modification

getTranscriptDirectory() and readTranscripts() together encapsulate the filesystem layout concern, meaning the base class has no hardcoded paths and each adapter can target a completely different directory structure

entities.name: PublicCloudProviders
observations: The parent component description references a 'public' LLMMode value, indicating cloud providers are the default fallback when no override or global mode is set in getLLMMode()
Provider configuration is declared in config/llm-providers.yaml, suggesting each cloud provider adapter reads its endpoint, model list, and credentials from that YAML rather than hardcoding them
The TierRouter component routes to these providers based on task complexity, implying each adapter must expose a common interface that TierRouter can call uniformly
The DMR provider is described as living alongside these adapters in src/providers/, indicating a shared directory convention where each provider is a separate module file
The LLMMode union type 'public' in src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts maps specifically to these cloud adapters, meaning getLLMMode() returning 'public' causes the runtime to delegate to this sub-component
integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis docs reference multiple model tiers across providers, implying each adapter must surface model-tier metadata consumable by TierRouter

entities.name: LLMProviderConfig
observations: config/llm-providers.yaml serves as the canonical registry of provider definitions, meaning adding a new provider requires an entry here before any adapter code is wired up
config/dmr-config.yaml provides DMR-specific overrides separate from public cloud config, suggesting the config layer supports per-provider config files rather than a single monolithic file
Environment variable expansion is documented as a feature of this layer, meaning secrets like API keys are never stored directly in YAML but referenced via ${ENV_VAR} tokens resolved at load time
Multi-path discovery allows the config loader to search several filesystem locations, enabling the same codebase to resolve configs differently in development vs. containerized deployment

The LLMState interface and LLMMode type defined in src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts are consumed alongside provider config, creating an implicit coupling between runtime mode resolution and config-declared providers
integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md references provider configuration, indicating this config layer is also consumed by MCP integration paths

entities.name: ServiceProbeLibrary
observations: lib/utils/service-probe.js implements both HTTP and TCP probe variants under a unified interface, allowing health-coordinator to invoke either protocol without branching logic at the call site

The SPEC R6 contract explicitly forbids the probe from ever returning a 'healthy' result, meaning consumers like health-coordinator must interpret probe output as liveness signals rather than full health verdicts
health-coordinator.js consumes service-probe.js on a fixed 5-second tick interval, establishing a polling-based (not event-driven) liveness detection model

By normalizing HTTP and TCP probes behind a single library, service-probe.js acts as an abstraction boundary — adding a new protocol requires changes only in lib/utils/service-probe.js rather than in each health-coordinator call site
The 'never returns healthy' contract means downstream orchestration logic must treat probe results as binary liveness (reachable/unreachable) rather than multi-state health classifications

entities.name: ServiceStarterLibrary
observations: lib/service-starter.js implements exponential backoff between retry attempts, preventing thundering-herd restarts when a dependent service (e.g., database) is slow to become available

The library distinguishes between 'required' and 'optional' service classifications, allowing callers to specify that a startup failure in an optional service should not abort the overall container initialization sequence

Health verification is built into the startup loop in lib/service-starter.js, meaning the library does not consider a service started until a probe confirms reachability — integrating with the ServiceProbeLibrary's liveness model
Timeout configuration is a first-class parameter in lib/service-starter.js, enabling per-service deadline enforcement independent of the global retry count

By centralizing retry logic in lib/service-starter.js, individual wrapper scripts (api-service.js, dashboard-service.js) avoid duplicating backoff state machines, keeping wrapper scripts focused on spawn-then-register sequencing

entities.name: LLMMockService
observations: llm-mock-service.ts persists LLM mode state to workflow-progress.json rather than keeping it in memory, making mode selection survive process restarts within the Docker environment
The module supports three distinct modes — mock, local, and public — allowing developers to swap between fully synthetic responses, a local model, and production LLM without code changes
Per-agent overrides are supported, meaning individual agents within mcp-server-semantic-analysis can operate in different LLM modes simultaneously, read from the same workflow-progress.json file
The module is described as Docker-aware, implying it accounts for containerized path resolution or environment variable differences when locating workflow-progress.json
LLMMockService is architecturally separate from the PSM/wrapper pattern used by the other DockerizedServices sub-components, acting as a configuration provider rather than a process lifecycle manager

entities.name: KnowledgeMigration
observations: scripts/migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js consolidates entity types to a canonical three-value set (System/Project/Pattern), indicating the graph schema has undergone at least one breaking taxonomy change that required a retroactive data migration

scripts/migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs implements a full storage backend migration from raw LevelDB to the km-core canonical shape, suggesting a major architectural shift in how graph data is physically stored and accessed

The LevelDB-to-km-core migration in migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs includes dry-run mode, idempotency guarantees, and error-budget controls—a production-safety pattern that allows partial reruns without double-applying transformations

The existence of two separate migration scripts implies a sequencing concern: entity-type consolidation (migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js) may need to run before or after the storage backend migration (migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs) depending on which schema version each script expects as input

KnowledgeMigration operates outside the normal GraphDatabaseAdapter routing path—migration scripts likely access LevelDB directly rather than through the probe-once adapter, since the adapter's lock-free design would conflict with the migration process also holding LevelDB file locks

entities.name: AgentLifecyclePatterns
observations: BaseAgent subclasses documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md all follow a constructor(repoPath, team) signature that captures only configuration context, explicitly forbidding any LLM client instantiation at this stage.

ensureLLMInitialized() is implemented as an idempotent guard — repeated calls are safe by design, with only the first invocation allocating the LLM client, preventing double-initialization in reentrant or harness-driven scenarios.
execute(input) is the sole public entry point for agent work as specified in agents.md, and is permitted to call ensureLLMInitialized() at its top if a harness has not already done so explicitly.

The pattern is motivated by orchestrator scenarios that pre-instantiate agents in bulk (described in agents.md), where only a subset are ultimately invoked — lazy init prevents connection overhead for unused agents.

New contributor guidance in agents.md explicitly flags acquiring LLM connections in the constructor as a contract violation that causes resource exhaustion in bulk-instantiation orchestrator scenarios.

The CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md file (integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md) references resolved lifecycle violations, indicating this contract was retrofitted after early mis-implementations.

entities.name: ServiceRegistrationPatterns
observations: scripts/api-service.js calls ProcessStateManager.registerService() immediately after process spawn, establishing the registration as the canonical signal that a service is live and trackable.
scripts/dashboard-service.js follows the identical post-spawn registration sequence, confirming this is a project-wide convention rather than a one-off implementation.

The pattern decouples spawn mechanics from liveness semantics: a process is not considered managed until it self-registers, allowing ProcessStateManager to distinguish spawned-but-not-ready from fully live services.
ProcessStateManager.registerService() is designed to be called from within the child process context, meaning the registration is a push model rather than the orchestrator polling for readiness.
The convention is documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md, which covers integration patterns between managed processes.
This pattern provides a uniform hook point for future liveness checks, restarts, or health monitoring by having all services announce themselves through the same API.

entities.name: AdapterAndWrapperPatterns
observations: GraphDatabaseAdapter wraps the Graphology graph library combined with LevelDB persistence, exposing a domain-oriented API rather than the raw Graphology or LevelDB interfaces directly.
OntologyClassificationEngine wraps @fwornle/km-core, providing a domain-specific classification interface that insulates the rest of the codebase from the external package's API surface.

Both adapters perform storage probing at initialization time (not lazily), as documented in the AvailabilityGuardPatterns context — this is a deliberate choice to fail fast rather than fail on first use.
The adapter layer is documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md, which covers how third-party integrations are mediated through these wrapper classes.
The pattern prevents third-party library API changes from propagating beyond the adapter boundary, confining migration work to a single class per dependency.
GraphDatabaseAdapter's initialization probe aligns with storage/graph-database-adapter.ts as the canonical file path, indicating adapters live under a storage/ or similar infrastructure directory.

entities.name: ConfigurationSeparationConventions
observations: config/agent-profiles.json holds runtime behavioral configuration for agents (model selection, parameters, capabilities), deliberately separated from topology concerns.
config/teams/*.json files encode team topology — which agents belong to which team — keeping coordination structure separate from per-agent runtime behavior.
config/agents/*.sh launch scripts handle process invocation details, isolating environment and startup mechanics from both capability and topology configuration.
The three-way split is documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md, which serves as the canonical reference for the configuration schema.
This separation means adding a new agent requires touching all three config areas independently: a profile entry, a team assignment, and a launch script — each change is auditable in isolation.
The convention prevents the anti-pattern of embedding team topology inside agent profiles, which would make it impossible to reassign agents between teams without editing capability-defining files.

entities.name: DocumentationConventions
observations: All architecture diagrams are stored as .puml files under docs/puml/ directories, as evidenced by the documentation listing showing integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/ containing multiple .md files that reference PlantUML sources.
docs/puml/_standard-style.puml serves as the single shared style source imported by all PlantUML diagrams, preventing style drift across independently authored diagrams.
The convention is project-wide, applying across multiple integrations (mcp-server-semantic-analysis, code-graph-rag, mcp-constraint-monitor) rather than being scoped to a single module.
Architecture README files (e.g., integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md) act as index documents referencing the PlantUML diagrams for their respective component areas.
The use of a shared _standard-style.puml import means visual updates (colors, fonts, line styles) can be applied globally by editing one file, reducing maintenance overhead.
The CONTRIBUTING.md in integrations/code-graph-rag/CONTRIBUTING.md likely references these documentation conventions as part of contributor onboarding requirements.

entities.name: AvailabilityGuardPatterns
observations: isServerAvailable() is called before dynamic imports of VkbApiClient, ensuring the optional external API client is never loaded if its backing server cannot be reached.
storage/graph-database-adapter.ts performs a probe-at-init check during adapter initialization, detecting storage unavailability at construction time rather than at first use.
The pattern uses dynamic import() calls (not static imports) for optional dependencies like VkbApiClient, meaning the module is never loaded into memory if the availability check fails.
Probe-at-init in graph-database-adapter.ts aligns with the AdapterAndWrapperPatterns convention, making storage initialization the canonical point of availability detection for graph persistence.

This pattern is documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md under integration patterns, indicating it is a recognized architectural strategy rather than ad-hoc error handling.
The two guard mechanisms (isServerAvailable() for network services, probe-at-init for storage) represent distinct availability guard strategies for network vs. local resource dependencies.

entities.name: AgentBase
observations: BaseAgent<TInput, TOutput> in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/base-agent.ts uses a template-method pattern where execute() is sealed and unconditionally calls all five abstract methods: process(), calculateConfidence(), detectIssues(), generateRouting(), and applyCorrections()

The AgentResponse<TOutput> envelope wraps both the domain payload and metadata (timestamps, confidence score, detectedIssues, routing hints), creating a uniform observability contract across all seven agents

All seven agents—GitHistoryAgent, VibeHistoryAgent, CodeGraphAgent, OntologyClassificationAgent, SemanticAnalysisAgent, ContentValidationAgent, and PersistenceAgent—must implement all five abstract methods with no ability to opt out, even for trivial no-op steps

The routing hints field in AgentResponse enables the orchestrating SemanticAnalysisAgent to implement conditional pipeline logic (skip persistence, re-run classification) without coupling to any agent's internals

The confidence score returned by calculateConfidence() serves as a pipeline gate—the coordinator uses it to decide whether to proceed to persistence or abort, as described in docs/architecture/agents.md
This design is documented as enforcing a 'consistent observability contract' in the architecture docs, meaning monitoring and logging tooling can treat all agents uniformly

entities.name: OntologyEngine
observations: OntologyConfigManager in src/ontology/ is responsible for loading and managing ontology configuration, making ontology definitions available to agents without requiring direct file I/O in each agent
LegacyOntologyAdapter provides backward compatibility for older ontology schema formats, allowing the engine to ingest pre-existing classification data without breaking the current type system
OntologyValidator enforces schema correctness before any classification occurs, ensuring that malformed ontology configs surface as configuration errors rather than silent misclassifications
OntologyClassifier in src/ontology/ implements the core classification algorithm, consumed by OntologyClassificationAgent's process() method to perform entity-to-type matching

The heuristics/index.ts module under src/ontology/ provides rule-based fallback classification when LLM-based or exact-match classification is insufficient, referenced in the TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md under docs/

The engine is designed as a service layer—agents call into OntologyEngine rather than embedding classification logic, preserving separation between the agent lifecycle (BaseAgent) and ontology domain logic

entities.name: CategoryGroupedRuleSet
observations: The RedactionSubsystem's core design decision, as described in the SubComponent context, is that .specstory/config/redaction-config.yaml partitions rules into named category blocks — this means the data model the RedactionRuleLoader parses is a map of category-name → [pattern list], not a flat list of patterns.

Because operators can 'enable or disable entire rule groups without modifying individual patterns' (SubComponent description), the parsed structure must carry a per-category enabled flag, making category identity a first-class concept rather than a mere namespace.

This design implies the RedactionEngine receives a filtered view of only active categories at startup (or on reload), so hot-disabling a category requires no change to the pattern code itself — only toggling the category's enabled state in redaction-config.yaml.

entities.name: LayerOrchestrator
observations: docs/puml/lsl-5-layer-classification.puml explicitly diagrams five discrete classification layers as a strict ordered pipeline, making the sequencing contract a first-class architectural concern rather than an implementation detail.

The parent sub-component description states 'each layer's output can inform subsequent layers,' meaning the orchestrator must carry forward accumulated state between layer invocations — a stateful pass-through design rather than a stateless fan-out.

Because the pipeline is ordered and each layer may depend on prior layer results, the orchestrator is the single point responsible for short-circuit decisions (e.g., halting further classification if an early layer produces a definitive result), as implied by the layered design in lsl-5-layer-classification.puml.

entities.name: AbstractTranscriptAdapter
observations: TranscriptAdapter in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js defines the five-method contract that all concrete adapters must fulfill, establishing a plugin-style extensibility pattern for agent integrations.

The separation of getTranscriptDirectory() and readTranscripts() as distinct abstract methods suggests the framework intentionally decouples filesystem path resolution from file parsing logic, allowing adapters to handle agent-specific directory layouts independently.

convertToLSL() as an abstract method indicates each adapter is responsible for translating its agent's native transcript format into a canonical LSL (Live Session Log) format consumed downstream by the LiveLoggingSystem.

entities.name: PollingCursorCycle
observations: watchTranscripts() in transcript-api.js establishes a setInterval loop that drives the recurring transcript ingestion cycle, with tick frequency configurable to control latency between transcript writes and LSL emission.

The lastEntryCount state variable persists across ticks within watchTranscripts(), enabling a slice operation that extracts only entries added since the previous poll and prevents reprocessing of already-seen transcript lines.

This stateful cursor pattern means the adapter framework is not purely stateless — each watching instance maintains its own read-position state, which has implications for restart behavior and missed-entry recovery if the process is interrupted.

entities.name: AnthropicAdapter
observations: The parent LLMAbstraction analysis identifies AnthropicAdapter as one of at least three concrete public cloud provider adapters (alongside OpenAI and Groq), all normalizing to a shared interface — meaning any provider-specific quirks (auth headers, message schema, response shape) are contained here and invisible to callers

The PublicCloudProviders sub-component is entered when getLLMMode() resolves to 'public', making AnthropicAdapter part of the default fallback path activated in the absence of any local or override mode — it is the most likely primary adapter given the project is built on Anthropic's Claude SDK

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md ('Tiered Model Selection Proposal') documents a tiered model selection design, suggesting AnthropicAdapter must expose Claude variants (e.g., Haiku vs. Sonnet vs. Opus) at distinct cost/quality tiers consumable by the TierRouter without knowing provider internals

entities.name: ProviderYamlSchema
observations: config/llm-providers.yaml (cited directly in the LLMProviderConfig parent context) is the single authoritative file that must be updated when onboarding a new provider — adapter code is blocked from referencing a provider until this entry exists, making the schema the enforced entry point for all provider registration.

The schema includes a tier-assignment field per provider, a design concern corroborated by integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md ('Tiered Model Selection Proposal'), which indicates provider capability tiers are declared statically at the config layer rather than derived at runtime from API responses.

Each provider entry bundles model lists and endpoint URLs together, meaning the schema acts simultaneously as a routing table and a model capability manifest — a developer changing a provider's base URL or supported models has exactly one file to edit.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md ('Configuration Reference') documents how YAML fields map to runtime behavior, suggesting the schema fields form a strict contract that downstream LLM abstraction adapters depend on, so unrecognized or malformed fields would break adapter initialization.

entities.name: TierRoutingTable
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md is explicitly identified as the authoritative design document for tier selection strategy, making it the canonical source for understanding how tier labels translate to provider+model pairs.
