The parent component analysis notes that tier labels map to 'specific provider+model combinations declared in llm-providers.yaml', indicating a two-layer indirection: tier label → routing table entry → provider configuration, so changing a provider requires only updating the routing table rather than every call site.

Decoupling tier semantics from provider names means a tier like 'powerful' can be re-pointed to a different model without modifying upstream classification logic, following an open/closed design consistent with the broader LLMAbstraction layer.

entities.name: AgentTierPolicy
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md describes the tier selection strategy that this policy layer sits atop of, providing the override hooks referenced in the parent component's characterization of TierRouter.

The parent analysis explicitly draws an analogy to 'perAgentOverrides in LLMState', suggesting AgentTierPolicy follows the same registry-style pattern where agent identity (likely an agent ID or role string) is the lookup key against a policy map.

Because overrides are resolved before the TierRoutingTable lookup, an agent pinned to 'balanced' will always route to the balanced model even if the task classifier would have promoted it to 'powerful', establishing a priority order: agent policy > complexity classifier > default tier.

entities.name: LLMStateStore
observations: LLMState is declared in src/mock/llm-mock-service.ts, a file whose name implies test-only scope — the SubComponent description explicitly warns new developers to treat this as a core types file, signaling a naming/location discrepancy that has caused onboarding confusion.

The perAgentOverrides map within LLMState enables individual agents to run in a different LLMMode than the globalMode, which means the system supports heterogeneous mode configurations across agents within a single process.

globalMode in LLMState serves as the fallback when no per-agent entry exists in perAgentOverrides, establishing a two-tier configuration hierarchy that getLLMMode() consults as part of its priority chain.

Because LLMState is described as both in-memory and persisted, developers must account for state hydration on startup — a mismatch between persisted state and environment configuration could cause getLLMMode() to resolve an unintended mode after a restart.

entities.name: UnifiedProbeInterface
observations: Based on the L2 description, service-probe.js exposes a unified interface so health-coordinator callers never need to conditionally select between HTTP or TCP probe implementations.

The design decision to co-locate both HttpProbeExecutor and TcpProbeExecutor under one module boundary means protocol selection is encapsulated inside service-probe.js rather than pushed to consumers.

UnifiedProbeInterface, exported from service-probe.js, provides a single abstraction over HTTP and TCP probe implementations so that health-coordinator can invoke either protocol through a common call site without conditional branching.

entities.name: ExponentialBackoffRetryPolicy
observations: lib/service-starter.js owns the backoff timing logic as a shared library concern rather than embedding sleep intervals at individual call sites, so every service started through the library inherits the same retry curve automatically

The policy directly targets the thundering-herd problem: by exponentially increasing wait time between attempts, it avoids multiple dependents simultaneously hammering a recovering service (e.g., a database restarting under load)
Centralising the backoff in a single file (lib/service-starter.js) means tuning base delay, multiplier, or cap requires a single-point change that propagates uniformly to all consumers of the library

entities.name: WorkflowProgressReader
observations: llm-mock-service.ts constructs the workflow-progress.json path by joining the CODING_ROOT environment variable with the relative filename, making the service portable across any Docker volume mount layout without code changes.

Because path resolution is driven by CODING_ROOT at runtime, the same Docker image can be pointed at different project roots simply by changing the environment variable, which is the portability guarantee described in the sub-component summary.

The reader must parse the resulting JSON synchronously or asynchronously before any mock/live routing decision is made, meaning it sits at the very start of every LLM call path in llm-mock-service.ts.

entities.name: MockModeGate
observations: llm-mock-service.ts acts as the single entry point for LLM invocations within the Dockerized service, so the gate is the only place where the mock/live decision is enforced rather than scattered across callers.

The gate evaluates a global mode flag read from workflow-progress.json and, according to the parent component description, also checks per-agent override fields before falling back to the global flag, giving fine-grained control over which agents use mocks during a workflow run.

Routing to mock fixtures rather than live backends avoids network calls and API costs during development or CI runs inside Docker, which is the primary operational motivation for the gate's existence in the Dockerized service layer.

entities.name: ProbeOnceAdapterRouting
observations: storage/graph-database-adapter.ts contains the probe-once initialization logic: on first use the adapter checks which backend is reachable and caches the result, so the routing decision (VKB HTTP API vs. GraphDatabaseService) is made exactly once per process lifetime rather than per request.

All hand-crafted ManualLearning writes (entity creation, observation attachment) are funnelled exclusively through GraphDatabaseAdapter, making it the single authoritative gateway that enforces the cached routing decision for every manual knowledge-graph mutation.

The dual-target design (VKB HTTP API path vs. direct GraphDatabaseService path) means a developer adding a new manual-write operation must only target the GraphDatabaseAdapter interface; the probe result transparently selects the concrete backend without changes to calling code.

Because the probe runs once and its outcome is stored, any backend availability change after startup is invisible to ManualLearning—a restart is required to re-evaluate routing, which is an important operational constraint for deployments that switch between VKB HTTP and direct-graph modes.

entities.name: CodeAnalysisBridge
observations: The parent component analysis explicitly identifies CodeAnalysisBridge as the stage that maps static analysis outputs to graph entity/relationship types including DEFINES and DEPENDS_ON_EXTERNAL, both confirmed as key documented components in the project.

The integrations/code-graph-rag/README.md (titled 'Graph-Code: A Graph-Based RAG System for Any Codebases') describes the broader code-to-graph infrastructure that CodeAnalysisBridge feeds into, establishing the RAG layer as a downstream consumer of its relationship data.

Relationship types CONTAINS_PACKAGE, CONTAINS_FOLDER, CONTAINS_FILE, and CONTAINS_MODULE (listed as key documented components) indicate CodeAnalysisBridge also produces structural containment edges in addition to semantic relationships like DEFINES and DEPENDS_ON_EXTERNAL.

As one of three parallel source channels in the batch pipeline, CodeAnalysisBridge must conform its output schema to the contract accepted by GraphDatabaseAdapter, meaning any new relationship type it introduces must be registered in the shared graph schema before use.

entities.name: EntityTypeConsolidation
observations: scripts/migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js enforces a closed, six-member canonical type set (Project, Component, SubComponent, Pattern, Detail, System), meaning any node whose legacy type label does not map to one of these six values must either be remapped or flagged — the finite set is a deliberate ontology constraint, not a convention.

The migration operates by mutating Graphology node attributes in-place rather than rebuilding the graph, which means the graph topology (edges, adjacency) is preserved while only the entity-type metadata field is rewritten — a targeted, non-destructive migration strategy.

Because the sub-component description references only migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js and not migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs, EntityTypeConsolidation is scoped exclusively to the Graphology in-memory graph layer; LevelDB reshaping is handled by a separate migration script and should not be conflated with this consolidation logic.

The presence of legacy type names as the migration input implies the project accumulated informal or experimental type strings over time; this script acts as a one-time (or idempotent) normalization pass that makes downstream graph queries type-safe by guaranteeing every node carries one of the six known discriminants.

entities.name: SingleProbeRoutingDecision
observations: storage/graph-database-adapter.ts calls isServerAvailable() exactly once during initialize(), making this the sole input to the routing decision for the adapter instance's lifetime — a deliberate design that trades adaptability for simplicity and predictability.

The result of isServerAvailable() is cached after the first initialize() call; subsequent method invocations on the adapter use this stored value without re-probing, meaning a server that goes down after initialization will still be treated as 'available' by that instance.

Because the probe is bound to initialize() rather than to individual read/write operations, the GraphDatabaseAdapter behaves as a statically-routed proxy after construction, with no circuit-breaker or re-routing mechanism visible from the parent context.

entities.name: LazyVkbClientImport
observations: storage/graph-database-adapter.ts uses a dynamic import (import()) for VkbApiClient inside initialize() rather than a static top-level import, so the VKB client module is never loaded in environments where the VKB server is absent at startup.

The dynamic import is conditional on the result of isServerAvailable(): when the probe confirms the server is reachable, VkbApiClient is imported and all subsequent adapter calls are delegated to it via HTTP; when the probe returns false, the import is skipped entirely and GraphDatabaseService/LevelDB is used instead.

This lazy-loading pattern means the adapter's module graph has a hard dependency only on GraphDatabaseService/LevelDB at static analysis time; the VkbApiClient dependency is invisible to bundlers and type-checkers unless they handle dynamic imports explicitly.

entities.name: LevelDbToKmCoreTransfer
observations: Resides at scripts/migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs (per parent component analysis); the .mjs extension indicates it uses ES module syntax, which constrains how it can import CommonJS dependencies and affects toolchain compatibility during migration runs.

The 'record-by-record transfer' pattern described in the parent analysis implies sequential rather than bulk processing, likely to enable per-entry error handling and avoid all-or-nothing failures across potentially large LevelDB datasets.

The parent analysis attributes dry-run flag, idempotency checks, and error-budget enforcement to this same file, meaning the safety controls and the transfer logic are co-located rather than split into separate modules—a design that simplifies invocation but increases the file's responsibility surface.

entities.name: EnsureLLMInitializedGuard
observations: Because agents.md mandates that constructors never instantiate LLM clients, the architecture requires a separate initialization pathway; the parent component analysis identifies ensureLLMInitialized() in agents.md as that deferred allocation point.

The method implements an idempotency check — verifying whether initialization has already occurred before allocating resources — preventing redundant LLM client creation across repeated calls within the same agent instance.

This guard pattern creates a clear two-phase agent lifecycle: a cheap construction phase (repoPath/team capture only) followed by a lazy resource-acquisition phase triggered on first substantive use, as described in the agents.md architecture documentation.

The existence of this guard is architecturally coupled to the execute(input) entry point: execute() implicitly depends on LLM readiness, making ensureLLMInitialized() a prerequisite that must be invoked (directly or indirectly) before any LLM-backed operation can proceed.

entities.name: BaseAgentConstructorContract
observations: As documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md, every BaseAgent subclass must accept exactly two constructor parameters — repoPath and team — establishing a uniform configuration-capture interface across all agent implementations.

The constructor contract explicitly forbids allocating any LLM client during construction, making object instantiation lightweight and side-effect-free; this is a named architectural guarantee in agents.md, not merely a convention.

By restricting constructor behavior to capturing repoPath and team, the pattern decouples object creation from I/O-bound initialization, enabling agents to be instantiated safely in contexts where LLM connectivity is not yet confirmed or needed.
All subclasses documented in agents.md conform to this same signature, meaning the contract is systemic across the agent hierarchy rather than an isolated choice in a single class.

entities.name: PostSpawnRegistrationContract
observations: scripts/api-service.js enforces a post-spawn, pre-work call to ProcessStateManager.registerService(), meaning no service logic runs before the registration handshake is complete.

The L2 description explicitly designates registration as the 'canonical signal that a service is live and trackable', establishing a system-wide contract: any service not yet registered must be treated as not yet live, regardless of process existence.

This pattern centralizes liveness semantics in ProcessStateManager rather than in process-level signals (e.g., PID existence or port binding), which means consumers of service state must query ProcessStateManager rather than OS-level primitives.

entities.name: ProcessStateManagerRegistrationAPI
observations: ProcessStateManager.registerService() is the single entry point through which spawned services announce their readiness, as established by the L2 description's phrasing 'canonical signal that a service is live and trackable'.

The method is invoked from scripts/api-service.js immediately after spawn, implying it must be callable very early in a service's lifecycle before any dependencies or request handling are initialized.

Because registration is the liveness signal, ProcessStateManager is responsible for maintaining authoritative state about which services are active — downstream components that need to route to or monitor services must consult this manager rather than probing services directly.

entities.name: DomainOrientedGraphAPI
observations: The AdapterAndWrapperPatterns description explicitly states the adapter exposes 'a domain-oriented API rather than the raw Graphology or LevelDB interfaces directly', indicating that method naming and granularity are driven by domain semantics, not library capabilities.

Project documentation references relationship types such as CONTAINS_PACKAGE, CONTAINS_FOLDER, CONTAINS_FILE, CONTAINS_MODULE, DEFINES, DEFINES_METHOD, and DEPENDS_ON_EXTERNAL (listed as key documented components), which are the kinds of domain concepts the adapter API is expected to model rather than generic graph edges.

This design isolates all Graphology and LevelDB import dependencies behind the adapter boundary, so swapping out either underlying library would only require changes inside GraphDatabaseAdapter without modifying any domain-layer consumers.

entities.name: GraphologyLevelDBBinding
observations: GraphDatabaseAdapter (described in AdapterAndWrapperPatterns) explicitly co-wraps two distinct third-party systems — Graphology for in-memory graph operations and LevelDB for on-disk persistence — rather than using either library in isolation, which is an intentional architectural pairing.

By combining an in-memory graph (Graphology) with a persistent key-value store (LevelDB), the adapter achieves fast runtime traversal while retaining the ability to reload or checkpoint graph state, a pattern common in graph-based RAG systems like the one documented under integrations/code-graph-rag/.

The co-wrapping design means consumers of GraphDatabaseAdapter are fully shielded from Graphology's node/edge mutation API and LevelDB's raw get/put interface, ensuring neither library's primitives leak into domain-level calling code.

entities.name: PlantUmlDiagramConvention
observations: The parent L2 context explicitly states that all architecture diagrams are stored as .puml files under docs/puml/ directories, establishing this as a project-wide convention rather than a per-integration choice.

The directory integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/ contains multiple dedicated Markdown files (agents.md, integration.md, tools.md) that, per the L2 description, each reference PlantUML sources — indicating a one-Markdown-per-diagram-topic pattern.

The architecture/README.md (titled 'Architecture Documentation - MCP Server Semantic Analysis') serves as an index into this diagram collection, separating navigation concerns from the diagram source files themselves.

Keeping .puml source files separate from rendered images means diagrams are version-controlled as text and can be diffed, which is a deliberate decision to treat diagrams as code rather than binary assets.

entities.name: LayeredDocsSubdirectoryStructure
observations: The integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/ tree contains at least three named subdirectories — architecture/, api/, and installation/ — each with its own README.md (e.g., 'Architecture Documentation - MCP Server Semantic Analysis', 'API Reference - MCP Server Semantic Analysis', 'Installation Guide - MCP Server Semantic Analysis'), making the subdirectory purpose self-describing.

Cross-cutting concerns that do not fit a single subdirectory are placed as flat files directly under docs/ (e.g., integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md and docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md), distinguishing stable reference docs from proposal/design documents.

The same convention appears in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/, which contains CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md, constraint-configuration.md, semantic-constraint-detection.md, semantic-detection-design.md, and status-line-integration.md — all flat files without subdirectories — suggesting the layered subdirectory pattern scales in only when an integration reaches sufficient documentation volume.

This structure allows tooling or CI to verify completeness: a valid integration docs/ directory is expected to contain at minimum an architecture/README.md and a top-level README.md, as demonstrated by mcp-server-semantic-analysis.

entities.name: AgentProfileRuntimeConfig
observations: The core convention established by this sub-component is that config/agent-profiles.json owns model identity and runtime tuning, while agent wiring and topology definitions live in separate files; this boundary is described as intentional in the Agent Architecture documentation at integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md serves as the authoritative schema reference for agent profiles, covering which fields (model selection, parameters, capabilities) belong in agent-profiles.json versus topology or integration configuration files.

Because behavioral config is isolated from topology, operators can swap models or adjust inference parameters by editing agent-profiles.json entries alone, without touching agent graph definitions or orchestration pipelines—reducing the blast radius of tuning changes.

entities.name: TieredModelSelectionPolicy
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md ('Tiered Model Selection Proposal') is a dedicated design proposal for this policy, indicating the model-selection strategy is a non-trivial architectural concern that warranted formal documentation separate from the general configuration reference.

The existence of multiple heterogeneous specialized agents (GitHistoryAgent, VibeHistoryAgent, CodeGraphAgent as named in parent context) makes tiered model selection architecturally significant: different agents performing different analytical tasks (graph traversal vs. narrative summarization) likely have very different cost-quality sensitivity profiles.

The configuration reference at integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md ('Configuration Reference') is the companion document that exposes the tiered model settings as operator-tunable parameters, separating the policy proposal from its runtime configuration surface.

entities.name: ServerAvailabilityPrecheck
observations: isServerAvailable() is explicitly invoked ahead of the dynamic import call for VkbApiClient, establishing a strict sequencing contract: availability confirmation must precede module resolution.

The guard treats VkbApiClient as entirely optional at the module level — if the server check fails, the import is skipped entirely rather than caught after a failed instantiation, avoiding partial initialization side effects.

This pattern encodes a fail-fast strategy at the dependency-loading boundary: the cost of a failed network probe is preferred over the cost of loading, parsing, and initializing a client module that cannot function.

entities.name: VkbApiClientDeferredImport
observations: By placing VkbApiClient behind a dynamic import guarded by isServerAvailable(), the sub-component ensures the external API client's module code is never evaluated in environments or conditions where its backing server is absent.

The dynamic import boundary means VkbApiClient's initialization errors, type registrations, or side effects are fully isolated — they cannot affect the host application's startup path when the server is unavailable.

This deferred-loading approach classifies VkbApiClient as an optional integration point rather than a hard dependency, which aligns with availability-guard patterns where external service clients should degrade gracefully to a no-op rather than crash the application.

entities.name: ConversationLevelInference
observations: As stated in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-constraint-detection.md (quoted in the parent context), the detector 'operates at conversation-level inference, meaning it analyzes intent and meaning across message context rather than matching surface-level text patterns' — this is the defining architectural decision of the sub-component.

The deliberate rejection of surface-level pattern matching (documented in semantic-constraint-detection.md) implies the detector must hold and process prior message context, making it stateful or context-window-dependent rather than a stateless per-message filter.

A companion document integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-detection-design.md ('Semantic Constraint Detection - Design Document') exists alongside the operational doc, indicating this inference approach was treated as a significant enough architectural choice to warrant a dedicated design rationale — a new developer should consult both files to understand the intent behind the conversation-scope design.

entities.name: ClaudeCodeHookIntegration
observations: Documented exclusively in `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md` ('Claude Code Hook Data Format'), indicating a dedicated, versioned data format specification rather than an ad-hoc interface — suggesting this boundary is treated as a stable contract.

The existence of a standalone format document (separate from the general README at `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md`) implies the hook payload schema is complex enough to warrant its own reference, likely defining required fields, event types, and envelope structure.

As the ingestion boundary between Claude Code and the monitor, this integration point determines what constraint signals are observable at runtime; any constraint that is not represented in this format cannot be surfaced by the monitor.

entities.name: CoordinatorAgentOrchestration
observations: The parent component context explicitly names GitHistoryAgent, VibeHistoryAgent, and CodeGraphAgent as distinct specialized agents all residing in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/, indicating a deliberate decomposition where each agent owns a single analysis domain (git history, vibe/intent history, and code graph respectively).

The documentation file integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md ('Agent Architecture') is a dedicated architectural reference for this agent structure, confirming the coordinator-plus-specialists pattern is a first-class design concern, not an implementation detail.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md ('CRITICAL Architecture Issues - RESOLVED') signals that the coordinator's sequencing logic went through a breaking architectural revision, suggesting the ordering or coupling between specialized agents was a significant design challenge that has since been resolved.

The integration patterns documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md ('Integration Patterns') likely describe how the coordinator hands off context between agents, which is a critical concern when multiple agents must share intermediate analysis results across pipeline stages.

entities.name: OntologyRelationshipSchema
observations: The project documentation's 'Key documented components' section enumerates the ontology's full relationship vocabulary: CONTAINS_PACKAGE, CONTAINS_FOLDER, CONTAINS_FILE, CONTAINS_MODULE, DEFINES, DEFINES_METHOD, and DEPENDS_ON_EXTERNAL — these are the classification targets OntologyClassificationAgent resolves entities to.

Containment relationships (CONTAINS_PACKAGE, CONTAINS_FOLDER, CONTAINS_FILE, CONTAINS_MODULE) form a four-level hierarchical nesting model of a codebase's structural layout, allowing the agent to express parent-child ownership chains from package down to module.

Definition relationships (DEFINES, DEFINES_METHOD) capture the semantic link between a container entity and the symbols it declares, enabling OntologyClassificationAgent to distinguish structural containment from symbolic authorship.

DEPENDS_ON_EXTERNAL is a boundary-crossing edge type that marks references to libraries or packages outside the ontology's internal graph, giving the agent a distinct classification outcome for cross-boundary dependencies versus intra-codebase links.

entities.name: BaseAgentFiveMethodContract
observations: The L2 context explicitly states that OntologyClassificationAgent in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/ 'implements all five BaseAgent abstract methods,' indicating a strict abstract class contract rather than an optional interface.

The architecture documentation at integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md ('Agent Architecture') is the primary reference for the BaseAgent contract, describing the shared agent pattern applied across the mcp-server-semantic-analysis integration.

Requiring all five abstract methods to be implemented enforces a complete lifecycle (e.g., initialization, classification execution, result validation, serialization, teardown) so that OntologyClassificationAgent cannot be partially constructed — a design decision that prevents incomplete agent states.

entities.name: EnrichedPayloadSynthesis
observations: Per the mcp-server-semantic-analysis architecture documentation, Insights operates downstream of classification, meaning it depends on completed outputs from at least two upstream agents before synthesis can begin.

The integration patterns described in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md suggest a pipeline where AgentResponse payloads are the primary data contract between agents and the Insights layer.

The agent architecture documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md establishes OntologyClassificationAgent and CodeGraphAgent as the two upstream producers whose enriched outputs feed into insight generation.

entities.name: OntologyConfigLoader
observations: Based on the parent context, OntologyConfigManager resides in src/ontology/ and centralizes ontology definition loading, abstracting file system access from individual agents in the SemanticAnalysis MCP server.

The architecture docs in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md describe agent patterns where agents consume shared services rather than performing direct I/O, consistent with this loader acting as a single read point for ontology config.
The design decouples ontology schema definitions from agent logic, allowing ontology updates without modifying individual agent implementations.

entities.name: SealedExecutePipeline
observations: In `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/base-agent.ts`, `execute()` is defined as sealed on `BaseAgent<TInput, TOutput>`, meaning no concrete subclass can override the top-level control flow or reorder, skip, or short-circuit any of the five stages.

The pipeline unconditionally sequences `process()` → `calculateConfidence()` → `detectIssues()` → `generateRouting()` → `applyCorrections()` with no conditional branching between stages, guaranteeing that every agent invocation always produces a confidence score, issue list, routing metadata, and corrections output.

This sealed design centralizes cross-cutting concerns such as error handling and observability hooks inside `execute()` while delegating all domain logic to the abstract methods, a pattern described in `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md` under Agent Architecture.
The unconditional invocation means `applyCorrections()` runs even when `detectIssues()` returns an empty result, so concrete implementations must treat a no-issue input as a valid, safe no-op case.

entities.name: FiveStageAbstractContract
observations: Declared in `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/base-agent.ts`, the five abstract methods partition agent behavior into typed semantic stages: data transformation (`process`), quality scoring (`calculateConfidence`), fault detection (`detectIssues`), dispatch logic (`generateRouting`), and self-healing (`applyCorrections`).

The generic type parameters `TInput` and `TOutput` on `BaseAgent<TInput, TOutput>` establish the typed boundary for the contract, with `process()` serving as the primary transformation step between them and the remaining four methods operating on derived or intermediate results.

Because all five methods are abstract with no default implementations, the compiler enforces that every concrete agent provides all five stages — there is no optional or partial implementation path, making the contract all-or-nothing for subclasses.

The presence of `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md` (titled 'CRITICAL Architecture Issues - RESOLVED') in the same package suggests the five-stage contract or its unconditional invocation semantics was previously a source of architectural tension, now resolved.

entities.name: TranscriptAdapterBase
observations: TranscriptAdapter in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js declares five abstract methods — getAgentType(), getTranscriptDirectory(), readTranscripts(), convertToLSL(), and getCurrentSession() — that every concrete adapter must implement, providing a single extension point for adding new agent integrations

watchTranscripts() in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js uses setInterval at a default 1000ms cadence, making the polling frequency a class-level constant rather than a per-adapter or per-consumer configuration

The polling loop in watchTranscripts() compares new entry count against the in-memory lastEntryCount cursor, meaning any adapter that reorders or replaces entries without changing the total count silently suppresses callbacks — a known correctness hazard
Callbacks are stored in a Set within TranscriptAdapter, so duplicate registrations are automatically deduplicated, but there is no priority ordering among registered callbacks

lastEntryCount is reset to zero only on adapter instantiation, not persisted to disk, so every process restart causes readTranscripts() to return all historical entries as 'new' unless the concrete adapter's own readTranscripts() filters already-processed entries

convertToLSL() is abstract, meaning the LSL schema mapping is fully delegated to each concrete adapter, allowing Claude Code and Copilot CLI to produce structurally different LSL representations if their implementors choose

entities.name: LLMStateManager
observations: getLLMState() in llm-mock-service.ts reads .data/workflow-progress.json at invocation time, enabling runtime mode switching without service restarts
setAgentLLMMode() in llm-mock-service.ts stores per-agent overrides that take strict priority over the global mode setting when routing LLM calls
setGlobalLLMMode() in llm-mock-service.ts sets the project-wide default mode, which itself overrides the hardcoded fallback of 'public'
llm-mock-service.ts maintains backward compatibility by reading a legacy boolean mockLLM field from workflow-progress.json, mapping it to the equivalent modern mode value
State is stored in .data/workflow-progress.json rather than environment variables, allowing mode changes to persist across process invocations without restart

entities.name: PublicProviderAdapter
observations: Provider implementations are located under integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/providers/, one adapter per cloud provider as implied by the three-mode architecture
config/llm-providers.yaml declares the set of supported public providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Groq), serving as the configuration contract that each adapter must satisfy
Each adapter is invoked only when the mode resolved by LLMStateManager is 'public', decoupling provider selection from call-site logic
The adapter directory structure referenced in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md implies a common interface that all public provider adapters implement
integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md documents provider-specific configuration keys consumed by these adapters at initialization

entities.name: HealthCoordinator
observations: health-coordinator.js consumes PSM state by name rather than PID, so service restarts are transparent — it never needs to be notified of PID changes in api-service.js or dashboard-service.js
The coordinator uses ServiceProbe's normalized status envelopes from lib/utils/service-probe.js to assess liveness, keeping probe implementation details out of the coordinator itself
Because health-coordinator.js only reads from PSM and does not own process handles, it has no signal-handling responsibilities — those remain entirely in the wrapper scripts
The coordinator aggregates health across all PSM-registered services, meaning a new service added via a new wrapper script automatically appears in health output without coordinator changes

entities.name: KnowledgeDecayTracker
observations: KnowledgeDecayTracker embeds staleness state directly in EntityMetadata rather than a separate store, so every entity read returns its own decay signal without additional queries
The invalidating_commits field records which git commits have been identified as potentially invalidating a given entity, linking code change history to knowledge validity
staleness_score is a numeric field enabling range queries and threshold-based decay policies, while staleness_check_at records the last evaluation timestamp for scheduling future checks
staleness_method field records which algorithm or heuristic produced the current staleness_score, supporting auditability and method comparison over time

PersistenceAgent manages reads and writes of staleness fields, centralizing the logic for updating decay state so that neither ManualLearning nor OnlineLearning write paths need to implement staleness logic directly

entities.name: EntityTypeMigration
observations: scripts/migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js consolidates legacy entity type names into the canonical six-type set (Project, Component, SubComponent, Pattern, Detail, System), rewriting node attributes in the Graphology graph
scripts/migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs reshapes raw LevelDB records to the km-core canonical shape, including populating missing ontology classification and bi-temporal staleness fields
Both scripts operate as one-shot migrations rather than ongoing services, designed to be run once against existing data stores before the new EntityMetadata schema is enforced

The existence of two separate migration scripts (graph-db-entity-types and leveldb-to-kmcore) indicates the migration was staged: first normalizing entity type labels, then reshaping full record payloads
Migration scripts must coordinate with both the Graphology in-memory graph and LevelDB persistent storage layers (via GraphDatabaseService) to keep the two stores consistent post-migration

entities.name: DynamicImportGuardPattern
observations: The pattern appears in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis where optional external services (e.g., Memgraph, external analysis servers) may or may not be running, requiring a probe before importing or invoking their client libraries

isServerAvailable() acts as a guard function called before any dynamic import(), ensuring that the import side-effect (network handshake, credential validation) only occurs when the dependency is confirmed reachable

Dynamic imports are deferred to call-time rather than module load-time, meaning the Node.js module graph for the mcp-server-semantic-analysis server can fully initialize even when optional backends like code-graph-rag are offline
This pattern is architecturally parallel to the lazy LLM initialization in base-agent.ts: both defer I/O-bound resource acquisition away from the synchronous initialization phase

The guard pattern enables the MCP server described in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md to degrade gracefully — tools backed by unavailable services return capability-absent responses rather than crashing the process

Conditional import blocks effectively create optional capability zones in the agent layer, where concrete agent subclasses only gain access to a dependency's API surface after the guard confirms availability

entities.name: LazyLLMInitializationPattern
observations: base-agent.ts in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/ is the authoritative source of the pattern, defining ensureLLMInitialized() as the single entry point for LLM client acquisition across all concrete agent subclasses

All concrete agents that extend base-agent.ts inherit the prohibition on constructor-level LLM instantiation, making this a structural invariant across the entire agent layer rather than a per-class convention

The deferred initialization solves a concrete boot-ordering problem: agents may be instantiated during application startup in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis before environment variables (LLM provider credentials, endpoint URLs) are fully resolved

ensureLLMInitialized() follows an idempotent guard pattern — it is safe to call at the top of multiple methods without re-instantiating the client, implying internal state tracking (e.g., a nullable client field checked before construction)

The testability benefit is explicit in the architecture: unit tests can construct any agent subclass, inject mock LLM clients, and control exactly when ensureLLMInitialized() resolves, achieving full test isolation without outbound calls

Placing LLM construction in a constructor is explicitly categorized as an anti-pattern in this codebase, violating the separation between object graph initialization and I/O-bound resource acquisition documented in CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md

entities.name: ConstraintMonitorDashboard
observations: Lives in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/ with its own README.md, indicating it is a self-contained UI sub-project within the broader mcp-constraint-monitor integration
The dashboard/README.md title 'or' suggests it may present alternative views or toggle between violation history and session statistics
Depends on persisted violation data written by the hook manager during live sessions, meaning it operates asynchronously from constraint enforcement rather than inline

Co-located with status-line-integration.md (integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/status-line-integration.md), suggesting the dashboard has a companion lightweight status-line display mode for editor integration
Described as visualizing 'session statistics', implying it aggregates violation counts, rule hit rates, or temporal patterns across multiple Claude Code sessions

entities.name: HookConfigurationLoader
observations: The two-level configuration model (user-level and project-level hooks.json) is documented in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md, establishing a clear precedence/merge strategy between global and per-project rules
Constraint configuration schema is detailed in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/constraint-configuration.md, which serves as the contract for what the loader must parse and validate
The loader feeds the unified hook manager that intercepts Claude Code native hook events, meaning configuration loading is a prerequisite step before any pre-tool or post-tool hooks can fire
Separation of user-level and project-level configs implies the loader implements a merge or override strategy, allowing project-specific constraints to augment or override global defaults

entities.name: HookEventRouter
observations: Claude Code hook data format is documented in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md, defining the event envelope the router must parse for each hook type

Supports at minimum pre-tool and post-tool events plus startup/shutdown lifecycle hooks, as described in the ConstraintSystem parent component description and reflected by the CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md doc
Acts as the central dispatch point that connects loaded configuration (from HookConfigurationLoader) with both pattern-based and semantic violation detectors
Persists violations in real time for dashboard consumption, meaning the router is responsible for writing violation records to durable storage before returning control to Claude Code

entities.name: WindowLabelFileIdentityEmbedder
observations: Inferred from parent context (no source files available): the parent description states the window label is 'embedded directly in LSL metadata' and 'becomes part of the persisted file identity', meaning this embedder runs before the LSL entry is flushed to disk, coupling it tightly to HourlyWindowCalculator's output.

The design decision to embed rather than annotate at query time means downstream consumers can trust the label without reconstructing it from raw timestamps — reducing read-path complexity at the cost of making write-time correctness critical and any retroactive label change a file-rename or re-creation operation.

Inferred from parent context: since no entry can be persisted without a valid window label, the embedder likely acts as a mandatory pipeline stage in SessionWindowManager's ingestion flow, enforcing that every LSL session record carries a canonical '0800-0900'-style bucket string before storage.

entities.name: ExternalizedRuleStore
observations: The parent L2 description explicitly names '.specstory/config/redaction-config.yaml' as the sole source of redaction rules, establishing a single file as the authoritative rule store for all pattern-match and keyword-based redaction.

The parent description notes that redaction behavior can be changed 'without code deployment', indicating the YAML file is read and applied dynamically — likely at startup or on config reload — rather than compiled into the binary.

Because the YAML is the only rule source and an invalid file causes LSLConfigValidator to block all persistence, the file carries operational risk disproportionate to its size: a syntax error or schema violation in redaction-config.yaml becomes a full system outage vector.

entities.name: AbstractAdapterContract
observations: TranscriptAdapter in lib/agent-api/transcript-api.js defines these five methods as the sole extension point for adding new agent integrations, meaning any new agent (e.g., Claude Code, Copilot CLI) must provide concrete implementations of all five.

The separation of getTranscriptDirectory() and readTranscripts() as distinct abstract methods suggests adapters are expected to encapsulate both path resolution and file-reading logic specific to each agent's storage layout.

convertToLSL() as an abstract method implies each adapter is responsible for normalizing agent-specific transcript formats into a common LSL (Live Session Log) structure consumed by the rest of the LiveLoggingSystem.

entities.name: ModeResolutionChain
observations: `getLLMMode()` in `llm-mock-service.ts` opens by inspecting `llmState.perAgentOverrides` keyed on the requesting agent's identifier, making per-agent configuration the highest-priority tier and ensuring agent-specific settings always win over any system-wide state.

If `perAgentOverrides` yields no entry for the requesting agent, the chain falls through to `llmState.globalMode`, providing a single-point dial for changing the LLM backend across every agent that has not been individually overridden.

The third and final tier reads the legacy `mockLLM` boolean field, which activates only when neither `perAgentOverrides` nor `globalMode` is populated; this makes it a pure backward-compatibility shim that cannot override the newer fields, preserving function for pre-`llmState` callers.

Because the chain is evaluated at call time inside `getLLMMode()` rather than cached at startup, a change to `llmState` mid-workflow is reflected immediately on the next agent invocation without requiring a service restart.

entities.name: TieredModelSelection
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md ('Tiered Model Selection Proposal') is an explicitly listed architecture document dedicated to model tier selection, confirming this is a first-class design concern for the public provider layer rather than an ad-hoc per-adapter decision.

A tiered selection strategy implies that public adapters (residing under src/providers/) expose a shared interface parameterized by tier, allowing the orchestration layer to substitute a cheaper or more capable model without touching provider-specific adapter code.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md ('CRITICAL Architecture Issues - RESOLVED') suggests the tiering design went through a significant revision cycle, meaning the current tier selection logic reflects deliberate architectural fixes rather than the original naive implementation.

entities.name: ComplexityClassifier
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md ('Tiered Model Selection Proposal') establishes complexity-based tier discrimination as the foundational routing principle, making the classifier the entry point for every routing decision inside TierRouter.

The parent sub-component description states the goal is that 'lightweight tasks avoid expensive frontier models', which implies the ComplexityClassifier produces a discrete tier label (not a raw score) so that TierRouter can perform a clean lookup without further normalization.

Because TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md distinguishes tiers by task complexity, the classifier must evaluate task-level signals—such as semantic scope, required reasoning depth, or operation type—rather than caller identity, separating it architecturally from the AgentTierPinMap override path.

entities.name: AgentTierPinMap
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md ('Agent Architecture') is the primary reference for this construct, documenting how individual agent identities are associated with fixed tiers within the routing pipeline.

The existence of a pin map implies TierRouter implements a priority-ordered routing strategy: agent-identity lookup against AgentTierPinMap is evaluated before the ComplexityClassifier runs, short-circuiting complexity scoring for pinned agents.

Separating static agent-to-tier bindings from dynamic complexity scoring (ComplexityClassifier) prevents per-request classification overhead for well-understood agents and provides a stable override path for agents whose cost or capability requirements are contractually fixed.

entities.name: HealthVerificationRules
observations: config/health-verification-rules.json is the single authoritative source of monitoring configuration for scripts/health-coordinator.js; changes to this file directly alter which services participate in the next polling iteration without requiring code changes.

Each rule carries an 'enabled' flag that health-coordinator.js evaluates at the start of every 5-second cycle, allowing operators to suspend monitoring for a specific service without deleting its rule definition from the file.

Rules encode per-service probe parameters (e.g., endpoint, timeout, expected response), meaning health-coordinator.js can apply different check strategies per service purely through configuration, keeping the coordinator logic generic.

entities.name: HealthStateStore
observations: health-coordinator.js writes to currentState.services after each probe result is received within the 5-second polling loop, ensuring the store always reflects the most recently observed health of each service.

currentState.services decouples probe execution from health consumers: other parts of the system read from this in-memory structure rather than triggering probes themselves, preventing redundant or uncoordinated health checks.

Because state is keyed per service, any component querying health-coordinator.js can retrieve a specific service's health in O(1) without iterating the full rule set, making the store efficient for high-frequency reads.

entities.name: SpawnLifecycleHook
observations: The L2 description explicitly states registerService() is 'called immediately after a child process is spawned', establishing a strict ordering constraint: PID must be captured before any further orchestration logic runs.
By design, the hook is symmetric — registerService() on spawn and unregisterService() on exit — so the registry's contents at any moment exactly mirror the set of currently live child processes.

This lifecycle pattern means scripts/process-state-manager.js itself is stateless between calls; all temporal reasoning about a service's liveness is derived from whether its identifier is present in the registry at query time.

The hook boundary decouples the spawning mechanism (whatever creates the child process) from state tracking, so process-state-manager.js has no dependency on how or why a process was started, only on being notified via registerService().

entities.name: AttemptTimeoutEnforcer
observations: Identified through parent component analysis as residing in lib/service-starter.js alongside startServiceWithRetry(), the enforcer operates at per-attempt granularity rather than imposing a single global deadline, which allows the retry loop in RetryPolicy to remain responsive after each timed-out attempt.

The fault-tolerance guarantee advertised for startServiceWithRetry() in the sub-component description requires that individual spawn hangs are bounded; the AttemptTimeoutEnforcer is the mechanism that converts an open-ended blocking spawn into a finite, retriable failure.

Because lib/service-starter.js is the single entry point for all service launches, the timeout enforcement must be generic enough to wrap any spawned service, suggesting it wraps the spawn promise or child-process handle with a racing timer rather than relying on service-specific signals.

entities.name: TypedEntityAuthoring
observations: The parent context explicitly names six supported node types — Project, Component, SubComponent, Pattern, Detail, and System — indicating a fixed, schema-enforced ontology that must be respected during manual entity creation rather than allowing freeform typing.

Entity storage targets GraphDatabaseService as the primary backend; a LevelDB fallback is mentioned in the parent component description, suggesting the authoring path includes resilience logic that transparently swaps persistence layers without changing the caller's interface.

Manual entities share the same ontology classification fields as automated entities (per the SubComponent description), meaning TypedEntityAuthoring must populate fields like type, hierarchy level, and classification metadata even for human-authored nodes — enforcing parity between automated and manual knowledge ingestion.

entities.name: EmbeddingLinkedObservationRecord
observations: The SubComponent description specifies 'ontology classification and embedding vector assignment' as explicit fields on hand-crafted observation records, distinguishing ObservationCuration from simple text annotation and coupling it to the project's vector-search retrieval infrastructure.

Because manual observations must carry embedding vectors, the authoring flow for this detail implies a call to an embedding model at record-creation time — a non-trivial side effect that makes manual observation authoring more expensive than plain CRUD and ties it to the same embedding pipeline used by automated analysis.

Observations are attached to typed entities stored in GraphDatabaseService, meaning each observation record must reference a valid parent entity ID within the Graphology in-memory graph; this creates a dependency ordering constraint where the parent TypedEntityAuthoring step must succeed before observation records can be linked.

entities.name: BatchGraphWriter
observations: MEMGRAPH_BATCH_SIZE is listed as a key documented component in the project, directly controlling how many entity upserts are grouped into a single transaction against GraphDatabaseService (Memgraph backend), making it the primary tuning knob for write throughput vs. transaction safety.

The integrations/code-graph-rag/README.md ('Graph-Code: A Graph-Based RAG System for Any Codebases') describes the downstream graph system this writer targets, confirming that GraphDatabaseService is a Memgraph instance exposed for graph-based RAG queries — meaning write correctness directly affects retrieval quality.

As the terminal stage of the pipeline described in the sub-component definition ('before writing to GraphDatabaseService'), BatchGraphWriter must handle both create and update semantics for all six canonical entity types, since repeated pipeline runs over the same codebase should produce idempotent graph state rather than duplicate nodes.

entities.name: DynamicImportFallback
observations: VkbApiClient at lib/ukb-unified/core/VkbApiClient.js is loaded via dynamic import (not static require/import), which means the module resolution can fail at call time rather than at application startup — callers must wrap the import in try/catch or equivalent promise rejection handling.

The failure case is explicitly tied to a runtime condition (server not running) rather than a misconfiguration, meaning the fallback is a first-class operational mode, not just an error handler — callers must be designed to function correctly in both paths.

The fallback target is direct LevelDB access, which means VkbApiClient acts as an HTTP proxy layer over the same underlying data store, and the two access paths should produce semantically equivalent results for read operations.

Because the import can fail silently (from the caller's perspective), any caller that omits the fallback will experience a null-reference or unresolved-promise failure rather than a clear error message, making the fallback contract a critical correctness requirement documented at the L2 boundary.

entities.name: EntityCrudEndpoints
observations: VkbApiClient (lib/ukb-unified/core/VkbApiClient.js) serves the KnowledgeManagement component, so its endpoint surface must cover at minimum the entity types tracked by the knowledge graph (nodes, edges, typed entities) with distinct routes per operation.

Because the same data is accessible via direct LevelDB in the fallback path, the CRUD endpoints effectively mirror LevelDB's key-value operations over HTTP — a design that constrains the endpoint schema to remain isomorphic with LevelDB's storage layout.

The 'typed graph entities' framing (from the parent component analysis) implies endpoints are parameterized by entity type, not just by ID, meaning route structure likely encodes type information (e.g., /entity/:type/:id) rather than using a single generic resource path.

entities.name: EventDrivenExportSubscription
observations: GraphKnowledgeExporter subscribes to entity:stored events emitted by GraphDatabaseService rather than being called inline, meaning file export is asynchronous and does not block the original write operation — a deliberate eventually-consistent design decision stated in the parent component description.

Because export is triggered by events rather than synchronous calls, a burst of rapid entity writes produces a corresponding burst of entity:stored events that the exporter must handle gracefully, motivating the debounce coalescing pattern described for this sub-component.

The subscription model implies GraphKnowledgeExporter holds a listener registration against GraphDatabaseService; any developer modifying GraphDatabaseService's event emission contract (event name, payload shape) must also update this subscriber to avoid silent export failures.

entities.name: InvalidatingCommitLinker
observations: The parent analysis explicitly names the invalidating_commits field within EntityMetadata as the artifact this logic populates, establishing a direct dependency: without commit linkage, staleness scores would be purely time-based and blind to actual code change history.

The sub-component description ties decay signals to 'every entity read', implying that invalidating_commits must be updated incrementally as new commits arrive rather than recomputed in bulk, to keep EntityMetadata current without full re-indexing.

Mapping commits to entities implies a code-ownership or file-overlap heuristic: the linker must determine which entities are 'touched' by a given commit's diff, which requires access to both the git history and the entity-to-source-file relationship already modelled in the KnowledgeManagement component.

Because the commit list is stored inside EntityMetadata rather than a join table, the linker must serialize commit references (e.g., hashes or IDs) into a compact, embedded structure suitable for a single-record read.

entities.name: AvailabilityProbeFunction
observations: The probe targets port-based services: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/configuration.md documents both CODE_GRAPH_RAG_PORT and CODE_GRAPH_RAG_SSE_PORT as the reachability endpoints, indicating the probe performs a TCP or HTTP-level check against these configured ports before loading any client module.

MEMGRAPH_BATCH_SIZE appearing as a documented configuration constant (project docs key components) confirms Memgraph is treated as an optional, separately-managed service rather than a bundled dependency — the probe must succeed before batch-write operations referencing this constant are ever attempted.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md (titled 'CRITICAL Architecture Issues - RESOLVED') implies the probe-before-import pattern was introduced as a resolution to a prior architectural failure mode, likely a hard crash when an optional service was absent at startup.

entities.name: GracefulDegradationResponse
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/tools.md ('Tool Extensions') defines the tool-level contract that each tool must satisfy; graceful degradation is a required part of that contract so that the MCP host receives a well-formed response object regardless of optional-service state.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md ('Integration Patterns') describes the integration layer between the MCP server and external services such as the Code Graph RAG SSE endpoint (CODE_GRAPH_RAG_SSE_PORT), establishing that the degradation response must include enough context for a client to distinguish 'service unavailable' from 'analysis failed'.

The resolution documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md suggests the fallback was a deliberate architectural fix: rather than propagating an unhandled import error, the tool now returns a controlled response that allows the overall MCP server to continue serving other tools.

entities.name: EnsureLLMInitializedGate
observations: `base-agent.ts` in `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/src/agents/` centralises LLM client acquisition in `ensureLLMInitialized()`, meaning no concrete subclass may bypass or duplicate this logic — the method is the sole sanctioned path to a live LLM client.

The lazy pattern means the `base-agent.ts` constructor performs no I/O: LLM credentials are resolved only when `ensureLLMInitialized()` is first invoked at runtime, decoupling object construction cost from network-bound initialisation.

The agent architecture documentation at `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md` establishes that `ensureLLMInitialized()` is a lifecycle contract: all LLM-dependent methods in any subclass must call it as their first statement, making the guard a cross-cutting pre-condition rather than an inline concern.

entities.name: SemanticRuleEvaluator
observations: The existence of both a user-facing doc (integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-constraint-detection.md, titled 'Semantic Constraint Detection') and a separate internal design doc (integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-detection-design.md, titled 'Semantic Constraint Detection - Design Document') strongly implies that rule evaluation logic is architecturally complex enough to require distinct documentation for consumers and maintainers.

The parent component description explicitly contrasts this component's analysis with regex-based approaches, indicating SemanticRuleEvaluator applies higher-order intent analysis — likely LLM-assisted or structured semantic reasoning — when evaluating each rule against tool event payloads.

Described in parent context as operating on 'intercepted tool events,' this evaluator sits downstream of an event interception layer (consistent with integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md documenting the Claude Code hook data format as input), consuming structured hook payloads to drive rule evaluation.

entities.name: MockInjectionSeam
observations: The lazy gate in `base-agent.ts` acts as an implicit injection point: a test that pre-populates the internal LLM client field before calling `ensureLLMInitialized()` will cause the guard to skip real initialisation, giving tests full control over the client without environment credentials.

The `CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md` document in `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/` records that prior eager-initialisation approaches required credentials at construction time, making unit tests environment-dependent; the lazy pattern resolved this by moving credential acquisition behind the `ensureLLMInitialized()` boundary.

Because the seam lives entirely inside `base-agent.ts` rather than scattered across each concrete agent, a single mock strategy covers the entire subclass hierarchy — tests do not need per-agent shim code to avoid live LLM calls.

entities.name: ViolationClassifier
observations: The integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/README.md confirms a dashboard component exists as a downstream consumer of violation data, implying ViolationClassifier must produce a typed, displayable violation structure that the dashboard can render by severity or category.

integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/constraint-configuration.md ('Constraint Configuration Guide') suggests violations are configurable, meaning ViolationClassifier likely maps rule outcomes to severity levels or violation types that were declared in user configuration rather than being hardcoded.

Positioned at the output boundary of SemanticConstraintDetector, ViolationClassifier bridges the detection pipeline to downstream systems — referenced in the parent analysis as feeding 'persistence and dashboard display' — making it the key integration point where raw detection results are normalized into a schema shared across the constraint monitor integration.

entities.name: ViolationHistoryView
observations: The dashboard sub-project lives in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/ with its own README.md, indicating it is a self-contained UI component focused on surfacing monitor data to users.
The parent integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md describes constraint monitoring as a core capability, implying the dashboard must present violation records as a primary view.
The docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md documents the hook data format that feeds violation events, suggesting ViolationHistoryView consumes structured hook payloads to populate its log.

entities.name: UserProjectPrecedenceMergeStrategy
observations: The two-level configuration model is explicitly documented in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md, establishing that a user-level hooks.json acts as a global baseline that applies across all projects absent project-specific overrides.

Project-level hooks.json entries override or extend the user-level configuration, implementing a clear precedence hierarchy where per-project rules win over global rules — a merge strategy described in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md.

The integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/constraint-configuration.md ('Constraint Configuration Guide') elaborates the schema for specifying per-project constraint rules, indicating the loader must understand both file locations and how their fields combine or supersede each other.

This layered approach means a developer adding a new project only needs to supply a project-level hooks.json with delta rules, relying on user-level defaults for anything unspecified — reducing duplication across projects.

entities.name: HookEventEnvelopeParser
observations: The event envelope schema is formally specified in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md ('Claude Code Hook Data Format'), which serves as the authoritative contract this parser must implement.

The parent context states the document defines 'the event envelope the router must parse for each hook type,' implying the parser handles a shared outer wrapper structure common to all hook types rather than per-type-only schemas.

Because no source files are available, the parser's exact field mappings cannot be confirmed, but its existence is strongly implied by the HookEventRouter's documented dependency on CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md for parsing behavior.

entities.name: HookTypeDispatcher
observations: The parent context explicitly describes HookEventRouter as handling 'each hook type,' confirming that multiple distinct hook types exist and must be dispatched separately — this multiplicity is the dispatcher's reason for existence.

The format document integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md defines per-hook-type structures, suggesting the dispatcher uses the hook type discriminator from the envelope to select the correct handler branch.

Integration with the broader ConstraintSystem (L1) is implied by the sub-component's placement: the dispatcher's output feeds constraint evaluation logic documented elsewhere in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/constraint-configuration.md ('Constraint Configuration Guide').

entities.name: BaseAgentLifecycleContract
observations: Per the Pipeline sub-component description, BaseAgent exposes exactly five abstract lifecycle methods (process, calculateConfidence, detectIssues, generateRouting, applyCorrections), meaning any concrete coordinator agent must provide implementations for all five or risk undefined pipeline behavior.

The agent architecture documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md underpins this contract; the coordinator's role is strictly compositional—it chains these inherited methods in sequence rather than housing custom logic outside the defined hooks.

The separation of detectIssues and generateRouting as distinct lifecycle phases implies the pipeline models error detection and downstream routing as independent concerns, preventing routing logic from short-circuiting issue collection.

entities.name: AgentResponseEnvelope
observations: As stated in the Pipeline sub-component description, every lifecycle stage is required to emit structured metadata into the AgentResponse envelope, making the envelope the canonical contract between the pipeline and any downstream consumer or MCP tool caller.

The architecture overview at integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md contextualizes AgentResponse as the integration boundary for the MCP server, meaning external callers never interact with intermediate stage outputs—only the final envelope.

Because applyCorrections is the last lifecycle method composed before the envelope is returned, the AgentResponse implicitly carries correction-adjusted output, requiring consumers to treat its contents as post-correction rather than raw analysis results.

entities.name: OntologyClassReclassificationGuard
observations: The L2 parent context explicitly states that PersistenceAgent 'consumes [the ontologyClass field] to avoid re-classification', establishing a directional data dependency: OntologyClassificationAgent produces, PersistenceAgent guards — a pattern consistent with the integration patterns documented in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md.

The guard operates at the AgentResponse level rather than at a separate cache layer, meaning the ontologyClass field doubles as both a classification result and a classification-presence signal — the absence or presence of the field likely drives the conditional re-classification logic in PersistenceAgent.

This design prevents the Ontology sub-component from being invoked redundantly on entities already stored, which is architecturally significant in a graph-based system like the one described in integrations/code-graph-rag/README.md where repeated writes to the same node would be costly.

entities.name: ConfidenceBasedRoutingHints
observations: As described in the Insights sub-component contract and documented under `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md`, `generateRouting()` returns hints rather than hard directives, allowing downstream orchestration to aggregate multiple signals before committing a pattern to the report authoring stage.

The dual-path design creates a feedback loop: low-confidence outputs re-enter the SemanticAnalysis pipeline rather than being discarded, meaning the system iteratively refines ambiguous patterns across multiple agent passes.

By encoding confidence as a routing signal rather than a filter, the architecture separates the concern of pattern extraction (done by the agent) from the concern of pipeline flow control (done by the routing hint consumer), keeping individual agents stateless with respect to pipeline topology.

entities.name: BaseAgentGenericContract
observations: Referenced explicitly in the Insights sub-component description and elaborated in `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md`, `BaseAgent<TInput, TOutput>` uses TypeScript generics to enforce distinct data shapes at ingestion (`TInput`) and emission (`TOutput`) without coupling individual agents to a common payload schema.

Because `generateRouting()` is declared on `BaseAgent`, all conforming Insight agents are required to implement routing hint logic, making confidence-based dispatch a first-class concern of the agent contract rather than an optional add-on.

The generic parameterization allows heterogeneous insight agents (operating on, e.g., call-graph patterns, dependency clusters, or naming conventions) to share the same orchestration infrastructure described in `integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md`, while retaining strong type safety across the pipeline.

entities.name: TextPatternPrimitiveLibrary
observations: `src/agents/semantic-analyzer.ts` is described in parent context as 'the single authoritative source for text pattern primitives,' indicating it deliberately centralizes pattern definitions rather than allowing each consumer to maintain its own variants—a deliberate DRY architectural decision.

The file serves OntologyClassificationAgent, CodeGraphAgent, ContentValidationAgent, and SemanticAnalysisAgent simultaneously, meaning the primitive surface must be broad enough to cover ontology labeling, graph edge inference, validation rule matching, and general semantic tagging without specialization leakage.

Because four agents with structurally different responsibilities (classification, graph traversal, validation, analysis) all satisfy their pattern-matching needs from this single file, the primitives are likely compositional functions or regex/NLP building blocks rather than opinionated high-level routines.

entities.name: QuadAgentSharedDependencyPattern
observations: OntologyClassificationAgent, CodeGraphAgent, ContentValidationAgent, and SemanticAnalysisAgent all import from `src/agents/semantic-analyzer.ts` per the parent component description, meaning this file sits at the intersection of four separate agent execution paths and any breaking API change propagates to all four simultaneously.

The designation 'single authoritative source' (from the L2 hierarchy context) implies governance intent: new text pattern logic must be added here rather than directly into a consuming agent, enforcing a contribution choke-point that protects cross-agent consistency.

The span of consumers—ranging from low-level graph topology (CodeGraphAgent) to high-level ontology labeling (OntologyClassificationAgent)—suggests the shared dependency pattern was chosen to prevent semantic drift where two agents independently implement subtly different definitions of the same text pattern.

entities.name: RedactionLayer
observations: Redaction rules are declared in .specstory/config/redaction-config.yaml, externalizing privacy policy from code so operators can update patterns without modifying the adapter or converter logic.

LSLConfigValidator enforces a redactionConfig schema, meaning any misconfigured redaction rule (e.g., invalid regex pattern or missing required field) is caught at startup rather than at runtime when sensitive data might already be exposed.

Redaction is applied before persistence, positioning the RedactionLayer as a mandatory pipeline stage between convertToLSL() normalization in lsl-converter.js and the final write step, ensuring no raw PII reaches storage.

The pattern-based approach implies the redaction config holds regex or glob patterns matched against LSL typed-entry fields (user, assistant, tool_result, etc.), requiring the layer to iterate over all entry types produced by convertToLSL().

Because the redaction config is separate from agent-specific adapter code in transcript-api.js, a single redaction policy applies uniformly across all agent integrations (Claude Code, Copilot CLI, and future adapters), avoiding per-adapter privacy logic duplication.

entities.name: ServiceProbe
observations: ServiceProbe lives at lib/utils/service-probe.js and is consumed by scripts/health-coordinator.js, establishing a clear utility-to-orchestrator dependency direction
The module exposes both HTTP and TCP probe strategies, allowing health-coordinator.js to check services regardless of their protocol (REST API vs raw socket)
All probe functions return normalized status envelopes, meaning health-coordinator.js can process results from HTTP and TCP probes through a uniform interface without branching on probe type
Because health-coordinator.js queries PSM state via the ProcessStateManager registry rather than holding PIDs directly, ServiceProbe results are correlated to service identities not OS process IDs

The normalization contract between ServiceProbe and health-coordinator.js means adding a new probe type (e.g., gRPC) requires only implementing the same envelope shape, not changing the coordinator logic

entities.name: ConstraintAPIWrapper
observations: scripts/api-service.js uses Node's child_process module to spawn the constraint monitor Express API, decoupling the OS-level PID from the service identity tracked by PSM
After spawning, api-service.js calls psm.registerService() so that health-coordinator.js and other PSM consumers can reference the service by name without knowing its PID
SIGTERM and SIGINT handlers in api-service.js forward signals to the child process, making the wrapper the sole owner of signal-handling for this service
The exit handler calls psm.unregisterService() to clean up the PSM registry when the Express API process terminates, preventing stale entries from misleading health checks
The wrapper pattern means a restart (new child PID) is transparent to health-coordinator.js — only api-service.js updates the registry, fulfilling the PSM decoupling contract
Retry and restart policy for the constraint API is owned by lib/service-starter.js, not by api-service.js itself, keeping the wrapper focused solely on lifecycle signals and PSM registration

entities.name: DashboardWrapper
observations: scripts/dashboard-service.js mirrors the structural pattern of api-service.js exactly: spawn via child_process, registerService, wire signals, unregisterService on exit
The dashboard child process is started on port 3030 (versus the API on 3031), making port assignment a wrapper-level configuration detail rather than a PSM concern
SIGTERM/SIGINT forwarding in dashboard-service.js ensures that Docker container stop signals reach the Next.js process rather than leaving orphaned Node processes
Because dashboard-service.js calls psm.unregisterService() in the exit handler, a crashed dashboard is immediately reflected in PSM state without requiring health-coordinator.js to poll
The duplication between api-service.js and dashboard-service.js is explicitly a maintenance risk: adding a third service means copying this boilerplate rather than invoking a shared abstraction
Restart/retry logic for the dashboard is delegated to lib/service-starter.js, preserving the separation between retry policy and signal-handling responsibility

entities.name: EntityMigrationScripts
