observations: migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js handles type consolidation—renaming or merging entity type labels in the Graphology+LevelDB store without full data reconstruction.
migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs performs the canonical shape migration from raw LevelDB records to the km-core schema, including field normalization for ontology classification and bi-temporal fields.
Both scripts implement error budgets, allowing a configurable number of per-entity failures before aborting, so partial migrations can proceed without a single bad record halting the entire run.

The migration scripts operate on LevelDB directly (bypassing the VKB HTTP API) to avoid lock conflicts during large batch rewrites, mirroring the GraphDatabaseAdapter fallback pattern used in online learning.

migrate-leveldb-to-kmcore.mjs targets the six canonical entity types (System, Project, Component, SubComponent, Pattern, Detail) as the destination schema, enforcing the ontology hierarchy defined in GraphDatabaseService.

observations: The BaseAgent class in base-agent.ts defines the lifecycle methods init(), start(), stop(), pause(), and resume()
The ensureLLMInitialized() function in base-agent.ts serves as a lazy initialization gate for the LLM
Concrete agent implementations, such as MyAgent.ts, extend the BaseAgent class and implement the lifecycle methods
The AgentLifecyclePattern is referenced in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md, highlighting its importance in the overall architecture
The lazy initialization gate ensureLLMInitialized() helps prevent redundant LLM re-classification
The BaseAgent contract is used throughout the codebase, with examples in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/installation/README.md
The lifecycle methods are designed to work with the StructuredEventEnvelopePattern, as seen in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md

The pipeline stage outputs in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md enforce the structured event envelope format
The StructuredEventEnvelopePattern is used throughout the codebase, with examples in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/api/README.md

The diagrams are used throughout the codebase, with examples in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md

entities.name: OntologySubsystem
observations: OntologyConfigManager centralizes all ontology configuration loading under src/ontology/, meaning changes to entity type hierarchies flow through a single managed entry point rather than being scattered across agents

LegacyOntologyAdapter exists explicitly to bridge older ontology schemas to the current classification interface, indicating the ontology system has undergone at least one breaking schema migration that required backward compatibility shims

OntologyClassifier under src/ontology/ is the runtime classifier distinct from OntologyClassificationAgent in the pipeline — the agent delegates to OntologyClassifier, separating pipeline lifecycle concerns from classification logic

Heuristic classifiers supplement LLM-based classification with rule-based fast paths, reducing model calls for entity types that can be determined structurally (e.g., CONTAINS_FILE, DEFINES_METHOD relationships documented in project references)

OntologyValidator in src/ontology/ is invoked by the pipeline agent's detectIssues() slot, creating a clear separation where the subsystem owns validation logic and the agent merely surfaces results through the BaseAgent contract

The integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md (marked RESOLVED) implies the ontology subsystem was a prior source of architectural coupling that was refactored, likely the motivation for separating OntologyConfigManager from inline agent configuration

entities.name: HourlyBucketEncoder
observations: The L2 parent context explicitly identifies lsl-converter.js as a primary file that references LSLMetadata.timeWindow, making it the likely home of the encoding logic that produces strings like '0800-0900'.

The output format 'HHMM-HHMM' implies the encoder truncates a timestamp to its hour, then constructs both the start boundary (e.g., '0800') and the derived end boundary (start + 60 minutes, e.g., '0900') — an architectural decision that fixes bucket size at exactly one hour regardless of actual session duration.

Because both transcript-api.js and lsl-converter.js reference LSLMetadata.timeWindow (per the L2 description), the encoding logic must produce a canonical string both files agree on, suggesting a shared formatter rather than inline ad-hoc formatting in each file.

entities.name: SessionBoundaryDetector
observations: The L2 parent description references 'session time boundaries' encoded in LSLMetadata.timeWindow, implying that at least one function — likely getCurrentSession() in transcript-api.js — reads the existing timeWindow value and tests whether the current moment still falls within it.

The fixed 60-minute bucketing granularity noted in the L2 context means the boundary check is a simple arithmetic comparison (current hour != session start hour) rather than a configurable threshold, making the detector stateless beyond reading the active timeWindow string.

Because transcript-api.js is cited alongside lsl-converter.js as a consumer of LSLMetadata.timeWindow, the boundary detection logic in transcript-api.js likely triggers a call back into lsl-converter.js to seal the previous window record and initialize a new one when a boundary is crossed.

entities.name: RedactionConfigLoader
observations: The L2 sub-component description explicitly names .specstory/config/redaction-config.yaml as the canonical location for redaction rules, establishing a single configuration artifact that the loader must target at pipeline startup.

By loading rules from a YAML file rather than embedding them in code, the design deliberately separates the concern of 'what is sensitive' from 'how to redact it', meaning operators can ship policy changes independently of software releases.

The parent analysis references an LSLConfigValidator redactionConfig schema, indicating that RedactionConfigLoader feeds its parsed output through a validation step before any redaction is applied — preventing malformed rules from silently corrupting the pipeline.

entities.name: AgentOverrideRegistry
observations: `llmState.perAgentOverrides` is a keyed map where each entry associates a named agent identifier with a specific LLM mode string, allowing heterogeneous agent fleets within the same workflow to target different backends simultaneously.

`setAgentLLMMode` provides the sole sanctioned write path into `perAgentOverrides`, centralizing mutation so that all per-agent mode changes flow through a single function rather than being scattered across call sites.

Because `getLLMMode()` in `llm-mock-service.ts` reads `perAgentOverrides` before any other tier, entries written via `setAgentLLMMode` unconditionally shadow `globalMode` and the legacy `mockLLM` flag for the named agent, giving operators fine-grained control without touching system-wide settings.

The registry is part of the serializable `llmState` structure persisted to `workflow-progress.json`, meaning per-agent overrides survive process restarts and are restored when a workflow resumes, maintaining routing continuity across execution boundaries.

entities.name: StatusEnvelope
observations: StatusEnvelope acts as the contract boundary between lib/utils/service-probe.js (producer) and scripts/health-coordinator.js (consumer), decoupling probe implementation details from orchestration logic.

Because both HTTP and TCP probes must map their distinct outcomes into the same envelope shape, StatusEnvelope enforces a lowest-common-denominator status representation (e.g., a status field and optional error payload) rather than exposing protocol-specific response details.

health-coordinator.js consuming this envelope uniformly means new probe types can be added to service-probe.js without changing the aggregation logic, indicating the envelope is a deliberate extension point in the utility-to-orchestrator dependency direction described in the SubComponent metadata.

entities.name: HttpProbe
observations: HttpProbe resides in lib/utils/service-probe.js alongside any TCP probe logic, establishing service-probe.js as a multi-protocol probe registry rather than a single-purpose module.

Its output is a StatusEnvelope consumed by scripts/health-coordinator.js, meaning HttpProbe never surfaces raw HTTP response objects upward — it translates HTTP semantics (2xx success, non-2xx failure, timeout) into the envelope's generic status vocabulary.

As a utility function (per the utility-to-orchestrator dependency direction noted in the SubComponent description), HttpProbe is expected to be stateless and side-effect-free beyond the outbound HTTP call, making it safe to invoke repeatedly in polling loops from health-coordinator.js.

entities.name: PSMRegistrationLifecycle
observations: scripts/dashboard-service.js follows the same registerService-on-start / unregisterService-on-exit pattern as api-service.js, indicating a shared PSM contract across all Dockerized services in the project.

The unregisterService call is wired to the process exit event (mirroring the api-service.js pattern), ensuring the registry is cleaned up even if the dashboard process terminates unexpectedly rather than through a normal shutdown path.

By bookending the child_process.spawn call with PSM registration, dashboard-service.js guarantees that the registry entry is only live while the spawned Next.js process is actually running, preventing stale entries that could mislead health checks or orchestration logic.

entities.name: SignalForwarder
observations: scripts/dashboard-service.js wires signal handlers as part of its startup sequence (described as 'wire signals' in the structural pattern shared with api-service.js), meaning signal forwarding is set up immediately after the child process is spawned.

By forwarding both SIGTERM and SIGINT, the wrapper accommodates both Docker-issued stop commands (SIGTERM) and interactive terminal interrupts (SIGINT), mirroring the exact signal surface handled in api-service.js.

Signal forwarding is architecturally coupled to the PSMRegistrationLifecycle: a forwarded signal that terminates the child process will also trigger unregisterService, keeping the two concerns in sync without requiring explicit coordination code.

entities.name: PersistedModeStore
observations: llm-mock-service.ts deliberately avoids in-memory state for LLM mode, instead treating workflow-progress.json as the single source of truth — a design choice that prioritizes durability over performance within the Docker environment.

workflow-progress.json acts as a lightweight persistence layer shared across process boundaries; any process restart within the Docker container will re-read mode selections from this file rather than falling back to defaults.

This pattern implies both a read path (on service initialization or mode query) and a write path (on mode change) in llm-mock-service.ts, making the file I/O surface the critical coupling point for mode state management.

entities.name: AgentOverrideResolver
observations: llm-mock-service.ts maintains at least two tiers of mode configuration in workflow-progress.json: a global fallback mode and a per-agent override map, requiring a merge step whenever the effective mode for a specific agent is needed.

The resolver must consult workflow-progress.json on each lookup to honour any overrides written by previous process instances, since no in-memory cache is kept per the persistence-first design of LLMMockService.

A developer adding a new agent to the system should expect the resolver to return the global mode by default unless an explicit entry for that agent's identifier exists in the per-agent section of workflow-progress.json.

entities.name: SingletonModulePattern
observations: The singleton guarantee means that when api-service.js calls psm.registerService() and health-coordinator.js later iterates the registry, they are operating on the exact same object—a direct consequence of Node.js caching the first require() result for all subsequent importers.

No constructor arguments or dependency-injection plumbing are needed: each of the three wrapper/coordinator scripts simply imports ProcessStateManager and receives the shared instance, making the module system itself the coupling mechanism.

This design trades explicit testability (injecting a mock registry) for simplicity across the three-consumer topology; a developer modifying any of api-service.js, dashboard-service.js, or health-coordinator.js must be aware that their changes affect the same live registry seen by all other scripts.

entities.name: ServiceLaunchInvoker
observations: ServiceLaunchInvoker resides in lib/service-starter.js and delegates actual process creation to the wrapper scripts api-service.js and dashboard-service.js, keeping the starter itself as an orchestration layer rather than a direct process host.

By design, lib/service-starter.js owns no SIGTERM/SIGINT handling; that responsibility is explicitly assigned to the wrapper scripts (api-service.js, dashboard-service.js), so ServiceLaunchInvoker can re-invoke a wrapper cleanly without signal state leaking back into the starter.

The invoker operates inside a restart cycle, meaning it is called repeatedly under retry conditions — its interface with the wrapper scripts must therefore be stateless and re-entrant so that each launch attempt starts from a clean slate.

entities.name: NameBasedServiceResolution
observations: The HealthCoordinator sub-component description explicitly states that health-coordinator.js 'consumes PSM state by name rather than PID' — this is the central design decision that distinguishes it from a naive process-monitor approach.

Because the lookup key is a stable service name (e.g., 'api-service', 'dashboard-service') rather than an ephemeral PID, a restart of api-service.js or dashboard-service.js that changes the PID is fully transparent: health-coordinator.js continues resolving the same logical handle without receiving any notification.

This design creates a clean separation of concerns: the ProcessStateManager owns PID lifecycle, while health-coordinator.js operates purely at the service-identity level, never coupling its probe logic to low-level process metadata.

entities.name: PSMStateReader
observations: The sub-component description states health-coordinator.js 'consumes PSM state by name,' implying a periodic or on-demand read from the ProcessStateManager registry as the first step before any probe is dispatched.

By deriving its service list from PSM rather than a static configuration, PSMStateReader ensures that services added to or removed from the registry are automatically included or excluded from health checks with no manual update to health-coordinator.js.

This reader pattern keeps health-coordinator.js stateless with respect to service registration: it holds no authoritative list of its own, delegating service discovery entirely to the ProcessStateManager and reading only what PSM currently exposes by name.

entities.name: OntologyMetadataMapper
observations: PersistenceAgent (referenced in the OnlineLearning sub-component description) owns the mapEntityToSharedMemory() call, meaning ontology field hydration is a responsibility of the persistence layer, not the extraction layer — a deliberate separation of concerns.

mapEntityToSharedMemory() is invoked after entity extraction but before any write to storage, establishing it as a mandatory pre-persistence normalization step that ensures every entity carries complete ontology metadata.

Because the sub-component description names both the agent class (PersistenceAgent) and the specific function (mapEntityToSharedMemory()), this mapper is an explicitly designed seam rather than an incidental utility, suggesting its interface is stable and likely versioned.

entities.name: VkbLevelDbPersistenceFallback
observations: The sub-component description explicitly names two persistence targets — 'VKB HTTP API' and 'direct LevelDB fallback' — confirming a priority-ordered write path rather than a dual-write or eventual-consistency model.

The use of 'direct LevelDB fallback' implies LevelDB is embedded or locally accessible, allowing PersistenceAgent to bypass the HTTP layer entirely and write to disk, which preserves entities even during extended API outages.

This fallback pattern, as described in the OnlineLearning sub-component, means the batch analysis pipeline can complete a full run without blocking on API availability, deferring reconciliation or re-hydration to a later sync cycle.

entities.name: TypeConsolidationScript
observations: The sub-component description explicitly names migrate-graph-db-entity-types.js as the implementation file, scoping this script narrowly to entity-type label mutations rather than general graph restructuring.

The script supports two distinct consolidation operations on the Graphology+LevelDB store: rename (one old label → one new label) and merge (multiple old labels → one canonical label), as stated in the EntityMigrationScripts L2 context.

By targeting only entity type labels—not graph topology, edge structure, or node payloads—the script achieves 'without full data reconstruction' semantics, meaning records are updated in-place with minimal write amplification.

Operating directly on the LevelDB store through Graphology means the script must reconcile the in-memory Graphology graph state with the persistent LevelDB representation during each type relabeling pass.

entities.name: StartServiceWithRetry
observations: The function startServiceWithRetry() lives in lib/service-starter.js and is the single exported abstraction that consumers call instead of invoking bare startup logic directly, enforcing a consistent retry contract at the module boundary.

Its design follows the decorator/wrapper pattern: the core service-startup procedure is kept separate from the retry orchestration, allowing the retry policy to be reasoned about independently of what it wraps.

Because the parent sub-component description identifies this function as the defining element of the ServiceStartupPattern, it is the canonical reference point for understanding how the project handles unreliable service initialization.

entities.name: CanonicalTypeSets
observations: Governed and documented in `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/constraint-configuration.md` ('Constraint Configuration Guide'), which acts as the single authoritative reference for which type identifiers are permitted in the system.

The closed vocabulary design decision means new types cannot be introduced without explicit addition to the canonical set — any type not in the approved list is treated as a constraint violation by the monitoring infrastructure described in `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md`.

Semantic constraint detection logic, documented in `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-constraint-detection.md` and its companion design document `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/semantic-detection-design.md`, validates runtime usage against these canonical sets to catch drift at the point of code authoring rather than at deployment.

entities.name: MigrationScripts
observations: Explicitly identified in the `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/constraint-configuration.md` ('Constraint Configuration Guide') as the mechanism that enforces fixed canonical type sets, making them the primary gating control for vocabulary changes across the system.

By running migration scripts as a required step during schema changes, the ClosedVocabularyPattern prevents vocabulary drift from accumulating silently — any deprecated or non-canonical type in existing configuration must be transformed or rejected before migration completes.

The MCP Constraint Monitor integration (`integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/README.md`) provides the runtime monitoring layer that complements these scripts, catching violations introduced after migration in live code via Claude Code hook data described in `integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md`.

entities.name: HookEventEnvelopeSchema
observations: Specified in integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/docs/CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md (titled 'Claude Code Hook Data Format'), this schema defines the structured envelope that standardizes how hook event data is represented and transmitted to the constraint monitor.

The envelope format acts as the shared contract between the Claude Code hook emitter and the MCP Constraint Monitor integration (integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/), ensuring all consumers parse the same top-level fields regardless of the underlying hook event type.

By isolating the envelope schema in a dedicated format document (CLAUDE-CODE-HOOK-FORMAT.md) rather than embedding it in implementation code, the design allows the constraint monitor dashboard (integrations/mcp-constraint-monitor/dashboard/) and other consumers to reference a single authoritative format definition.

entities.name: VkbApiClientDynamicImport
observations: VkbApiClient (lib/ukb-unified/core/VkbApiClient.js) is loaded via dynamic-import rather than a static require or ES module import statement, meaning the module is excluded from the initial synchronous bundle and fetched/evaluated on demand.

Because dynamic-import returns a Promise, any code that consumes VkbApiClient must await the resolved module before accessing its exports, introducing an explicit async boundary at the point of first use.

Placing the dynamic import inside lib/ukb-unified/core/ suggests the deferred loading decision is encapsulated at the core-layer boundary, preventing higher-level consumers from needing to know whether the client is loaded eagerly or lazily.

entities.name: PumlArchitecturalSpec
observations: The SubComponent description explicitly names docs/puml/ as the designated directory for PlantUML sources, establishing a project-wide convention that keeps visual specs co-located with but physically separated from prose markdown docs

The project documentation tree shows dedicated architecture directories (e.g., integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/) with files such as agents.md, integration.md, and tools.md — indicating that diagrams are intended to complement layered, topic-scoped written documentation rather than replace it

By canonicalising PlantUML as the diagram format (rather than exported PNG/SVG binaries), the pattern makes diagram source diffable in version control so reviewers can reason about architectural changes the same way they review code changes

entities.name: TieredModelSelector
observations: The mechanism is captured as a first-class proposal in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/TIERED-MODEL-PROPOSAL.md ('Tiered Model Selection Proposal'), indicating it was a deliberate architectural decision requiring justification rather than an ad-hoc implementation.

The co-existence of integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/CRITICAL-ARCHITECTURE-ISSUES.md ('CRITICAL Architecture Issues - RESOLVED') alongside the tiered model proposal suggests the tiered selection approach was likely introduced to resolve performance or cost-related architectural problems identified during earlier iterations.

Given integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md ('Architecture Documentation') structures the system around agents, tools, and integration patterns, the tiered model selector likely acts as a cross-cutting concern that agents consult when choosing inference backends rather than being owned by a single layer.

entities.name: AgentToolExtensionLayer
observations: integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md ('Agent Architecture') is a dedicated document, signaling that agents have their own lifecycle, roles, and coordination patterns rather than being embedded utility logic.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/tools.md ('Tool Extensions') is a sibling document to agents.md, establishing a deliberate boundary between agent orchestration responsibilities and composable tool capabilities that agents can invoke.

integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/integration.md ('Integration Patterns') documents how this agent-tool layer connects outward to external systems, implying the extension layer exposes well-defined integration seams used by the broader MCP server infrastructure.

entities.name: DualScopeConfigResolution
observations: lib/agent-api/hooks/hook-config.js is explicitly designated the 'sole entry point' for hook configuration, meaning no other module in the codebase directly reads either hooks.json file — all access is funneled through this one loader.

The user-level config at ~/.coding-tools/hooks.json establishes a machine-wide defaults layer, scoped to the individual developer, making it suitable for personal tooling preferences that span all projects.

The project-level config at {project}/.coding/hooks.json represents a project-scoped override layer that can be committed to version control, enabling teams to ship shared hook policies alongside their codebase.

Because HookConfigLoader reads two physically distinct files, it must implement a merge or precedence strategy to resolve conflicts between user-level and project-level declarations — the resolution order (which scope wins) is a critical behavioral contract for downstream ConstraintSystem consumers.

entities.name: BatchAnalysisYamlConfig
observations: The Pipeline sub-component description explicitly names batch-analysis.yaml as the file where agent sequencing is defined, making it the single configuration point controlling pipeline topology rather than having order encoded in application logic.

By externalizing agent order into a YAML file, the pipeline coordinator can be updated to run new or reordered agents without code changes, reflecting a data-driven pipeline design pattern documented under integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/README.md.

The file works in conjunction with depends_on edge declarations per step, meaning batch-analysis.yaml encodes both the agent roster and the dependency graph in one artifact rather than splitting configuration across multiple files.

entities.name: TwoTierClassificationHierarchy
observations: The L2 sub-component description explicitly defines the two tiers: 'upper ontology defines broad abstract categories while lower ontology definitions provide concrete entity types' — this is the core structural contract that OntologyClassificationAgent depends on for all entity tagging decisions.

The architecture documentation at integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md ('Agent Architecture') is the primary reference point for how OntologyClassificationAgent traverses this hierarchy when classifying code graph nodes.

The two-tier split enforces a clean separation of concerns: abstract upper-tier categories remain stable across diverse codebases, while lower-tier definitions can be extended with language- or domain-specific entity types without breaking the upper contract.

The semantic analysis README at integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/README.md ('MCP Server - Semantic Analysis') contextualizes this hierarchy as a core design decision for the semantic analysis server, distinguishing it from flat-classification approaches.

entities.name: PostPersistenceInsightTrigger
observations: As stated in integrations/mcp-server-semantic-analysis/docs/architecture/agents.md, insight generation is explicitly classified as a post-persistence concern, meaning it does not intercept or transform data mid-pipeline but instead activates after KG writes are committed.

This design separates the write-path (raw pipeline ingestion) from the read-path (insight consumption), so Insights agents operate in a read-only capacity against the persisted Knowledge Graph rather than against volatile in-flight data.

The post-persistence trigger boundary means any failure or latency in insight generation cannot corrupt or block the primary KG write path, establishing a fault-isolation guarantee between ingestion and analysis concerns.

Consuming already-written KG data (rather than raw pipeline input) implies Insights can be re-run or retried against the same committed graph state without re-ingesting source data, enabling idempotent insight regeneration as documented in the agents.md architecture description.
